➢ Use case : Predictive downtime alerts using Aveva Connect

1. Aggregate MES production data and other lab data into

CONNECT data services.

2. Train AI/ML models on previous production runs to detect
potential downtime causing conditions when producing a
specific product.

3. Suggested corrective action to operations teams based on

detected conditions.

4. Provide stakeholders with greater visibility to

recommendations to avoid downtime when producing this
product.

• Proactively manage production through analytical guidance to  operations.

Coromandel Data Analytics Use-case

Nutrient Prediction in 28.28.00 Grade

Problem
• Needed to predict the hourly N&P nutrients in 28.28.00 grade  based on critical

operational parameters

• Needed proactive action by operators to avoid quality rejection

CONNECT data services

8 months data collected from November 2023 to June 2024.

Measures undertaken
•
• Critical process Parameter considered for model building.
• Considered plant stabilisation period i.e Ammonia flow > 3 TPH.
•
• Model training done and implementation under progress.

Best Operating ranges and Alarm limits considered for better accuracy.

CONNECT
data
services

Add data

Set timing

Evaluate
model

Start
Training

View
Results

Results
•

Potential benefit 2.75 CR/year

14

Coromandel Data Analytics Use-case

Prediction of Free Acid in Reactor Slurry of PAP1

Problem
•

•

Needed to predict the free sulphate in the reactor slurry
based on process parameter performance
To avoid process upsets which ultimately lead to long
shutdowns and impact phosphoric acid production and
more P2O5 losses.

CONNECT data services & AVEVA Advanced Analytics template PAP 01 free acid prediction

Measures undertaken
• Model developed with AVEVA Advanced Analytics

•

•

•

•

•

•

•

•

6 months data collected from July to Dec-23.

10 No's of influencing parameter considered for model
building.

Considered plant stabilisation period i.e SA flow > 550
LPM.

EDA performed and feature engineering applied.

Correlation heatmap analysis done.

Linear regression accuracy – 9.6%

Decision tree regression accuracy – 49%

Random Forest regression accuracy – 92%

• Model training completed and implementation under

progress

Results
•

Potential benefit of 1.76 Cr/year

15

Coromandel Data Analytics Use-case

P2O5 efficiency improvement in PAP-2 : Ball mill-Descriptive Analysis

Results
Ball mill – Descriptive analytics shown that
• Developing a ball mill efficiency improvement model and based on historical data after segmented to rock ratio wise found strong

cross corelation between

<200micron size Vs Ball mill vent fan amps

1.
2. Ball mill vent system temperature Vs Ball mill amps
3. Ball mill vent fan amps Vs Screw conveyor amps

• At a constant feed rate ,observed different patterns of critical parameters Ball mill  Amps, Vent system temp, Acoustic sensor, Bag

filter DP ,Screw Conveyor Current.

• Potential benefit 2.98 Cr/year

Critical parameters behaviour at constant feed rate

Critical parameters behaviour at constant feed rate

17

Coromandel Data Analytics Use-case

Evaporator Availability improvements

Problem
•

Evaporator downtime FY 23-24 is almost 42% of the
entire plant.

Measures undertaken
• Overall heat transfer coefficient has been calculated
to monitor the rate of heat transfer to give anomaly
on scrubbing.

• Carried out analysis for incorporating scrubbing date

info dashboard for  easy monitoring. Collected
operational parameters and meta data from critical
equipment and configured an asset framework (AF)
template in AVEVA PI System.

• Developed Dashboards for Plant Operations

Maintenance team to enable real time monitoring of
KPIs and for data insights.

•

Incorporated Heat transfer coefficient and various
KPIs into dashboard for ease in decision making and
performance monitoring.

• Created event frame notification for various KPIs

alerts levels.

530.25, 24%

Chemical / India

Coromandel Achieves Potential benefits of
1M$ through Process improvement

Challenge
• Process upsets leading to long shutdowns and impacting phosphoric Acid production

and more P2O5 losses

• Evaporator downtime FY 23-24 is almost 42% of the entire plant.
• Loss of product produced due to non maintenance of target quality parameters

Solution

• Development of advanced analytics models on enterprise data management model to
process production and process anomalies using AVEVA PI system, AVEVA AAA and
CONNECT Platform

Results
• Reduced Asset downtime by 50%.
• Reduction Raw material usage by 10%
• Improved non compliance of production batches through real time monitoring of

quality parameters and timely corrective direction.

OCTOBER 2024

Intelligent data-driven
energy management

Data usage in the energy project at Michelin

Nicolas Auger, Frederic Eyraud, Baptiste Andrieux

DISCRETE MANUFACTURING  |  FRANCE

Michelin progresses toward sustainability

Challenges
• Standardize energy data from multiple sources for easier use outside operations.
• Process huge amount of data.
• Make energy consumption monitoring accessible to everyone in the company.

Solution
• Use AVEVA PI Server and Asset Framework functionality combined with Michelin
solution (Mapib) to read, standardize and contextualize data from each counter.

• Process data in the cloud and expose in a standard way for users.
• Develop a mobile app to offer quick and easy data access to everyone.

Link energy data to its context

Relationship between counters

• We have defined a method to describe the relation between counters in the counter list.
• Local team can reproduce the exact schematics in the PI Asset Framework hierarchy.

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Adding a layer of intelligence

Context to scale logical tests helping decision making

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

• Model consistency check:

• C03 ≠ C04 -> Error

• Leak detection:

• C10 > C11 -> Leak

• Detect model modifications:

• B91 not Active -> Use Model 1
• B91 Active -> Use Model 2

• New counter installation:

• B92 > 20% of C09 -> Install New Counter

• Virtual counter extrapolation:
• B92 = C09-(C14+C15+C16)

Organizing energy data context

Position of counters

Production asset tree (hierarchy)
• Each Production Asset is modelized in the Equipment Tree

using templates to organize its production data
• Sensors
• Context information (Localization, IT references)
• Product identification…

Energy tree (hierarchy)
• Using PI asset framework Weak reference, we can link counters

and Production assets.

ML-based data quality

Isolation forest: Machine Learning algorithm
to detect unconsistent values

Provided by:
Martin Esnault
Data Scientist

Data scoring : Once a value is detected, we score it based on
its gap from the average (0 is Good, 1 is Bad)

Parallelize training of 30,000 models every two hours.

Keep only high quality data to eliminate signal noise.

More usage, same cost

• Total monthly cost :

Less than 5K€

• Monthly cost by factory :

Less than 350€

Infra optimization

Data quality parallelization

Aggregation mutualisation

Success in mixing

Monitoring production energy consumption (local)
• Reduction by 10% of speed on one process, without any
impact on products quality, causes a 10% reduction of
consumption.

•

Local optimization on process parameters (3 to 5%)

Benchmarking between machines (central)
• Find the best process parameters combination for the same type

of products (up to 16% of savings).

• Compare different machines on same type of production (up to

10%).

Success in curing

Steam consumption at cavity level (local)

• Calculation of steam consumption at valve level in PI =>
Allow calculation and follow of consumption by curing
press for 0 Capex.

• Overall, less than 10% of difference between the sum of
valve’s calculated consumption and real counter online
for presses (120 valves by line).

Benchmarking between machines (central)

•

Steam consumption at press levels allow us to compare
different machines on same type of production. (not
possible before without counting at press level)

DISCRETE MANUFACTURING  |  FRANCE

Michelin progresses toward sustainability

Challenges
• Standardize energy data from multiple sources for easier use outside operations.
• Process huge amount of data.
• Make energy consumption monitoring accessible to everyone in the company.

Solution
• Use AVEVA PI Server and Asset Framework functionality combined with Michelin
solution (Mapib) to read, standardize and contextualize data from each counter.

• Process data in the cloud and expose in a standard way for users.
• Develop a mobile app to offer quick and easy data access to everyone.

Results
• Standardized data is coming to the cloud on 8 thousand counters and 15 sites.
• Data is available for use by every person onsite for monitoring of energy

consumption.

• Benchmark between machines from different sites is available for central teams.

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

linkedin.com/company/aveva

@avevagroup

ABOUT AVEVA

AVEVA is a world leader in industrial software, providing engineering and operational solutions across multiple industries,
including oil and gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power and utilities, marine, renewables, and food and beverage. Our
agnostic and open architecture helps organizations design, build, operate, maintain and optimize the complete lifecycle
of complex industrial assets, from production plants and offshore platforms to manufactured consumer goods.

Over 20,000 enterprises in over 100 countries rely on AVEVA to help them deliver life’s essentials: safe and reliable
energy, food, medicines, infrastructure and more. By connecting people with trusted information and AI-enriched
insights, AVEVA enables teams to engineer efficiently and optimize operations, driving growth and sustainability.

Named as one of the world’s most innovative companies, AVEVA supports customers with open solutions and the
expertise of more than 6,400 employees, 5,000 partners and 5,700 certified developers. The company is headquartered
in Cambridge, UK.

Learn more at www.aveva.com

© 2024 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.




# SOURCE: vendor_aveva\ISM Validation Rev 1.3.pdf
---

Validation in ISM

Melinda Hemstead, Data Consultant

March 2021| Version 1.3

Unlock your Class Library / Standard

Unlocking your Class
Library reveals the
validation icon.

Details

Open the Details section
and click on Class Library
Policy Configuration. This
reveals the ISM validation
rules that will be
automatically applied at
Validation.
*Some of these rules can be
configured if there are some
that you feel do not need to
be applied to your Class
Library/Standard.

Validation Rules

Open up each of the sections you wish to
amend the rules on by clicking on the ‘plus’ sign
against the applicable section.
The rules that cannot be amended are greyed
out as are specific to ISM requirements.

Amending the Rules

Click on the down arrow to reveal all
available options. Amend to the
required status.
Revert to original settings to restore
the validation rules

Choosing your Validators

Select which Validators you
require to run against your
Class Library from the
Validator list (best practice
advises to choose 1
Validator at a time)

If you want to sign your CL
on successful validation –
ie without ‘Errors’ check
the box.

Click ‘Validate’ to run the
process

Class Library Showing Issues After Validation

ISM advises there are some
issues with the Class Library.
Click on ‘View Issues’ to
display the issues found.

The Validation Filters will
highlight and display Errors
that have been located.
If you wish to see is any
Warnings, Suggestions or
Hints that have been
located, simply check the
appropriate box and hit
Search.
This will bring in any of the
filters you have selected.

Exporting your Validated Class Library / Standard with Issues

Click on the Export icon and choose
the options you want to add to your
export.

You can choose to highlight only
specific issues the validation has
uncovered in the Class Library.
You can export thr full Class Library
including issues or only rows that
contain the issues
If you do not indicate Active Change
Tracking your issues will not Export.

Export

Issues are shown under the
_ACTION column on highlighted
tabs.
The Actions shown will be based
on your requested issues at
Export – in this case I wanted to
view any Errors or Warnings.

Issues

ISM will advise you of your issue by
hovering your cursor over the highlighted
Id.
The comment will indicate what your issue
is.

Here showing that an Air Conditioner has
no Physical Attributes

Issues

The issue here shows that
this Attribute – Asset Type
Reference – has not been
used against any area
within the Class Library,
therefore you may
question whether you
require this Attribute
within the Library.

Issues

The issue here is that
Unit of Measure –
gallons per minute – is
missing its symbol

Review Issues and Re-Import

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Revalidate

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Once the Issues have been
addressed and you are ready to
sign off the validated CL, check
the box and click Validate.

ABOUT AVEVA
AVEVA is a global leader in engineering and industrial software driving digital transformation across
the entire asset and operational life cycle of capital-intensive industries.

The company’s engineering, planning and operations, asset performance, and monitoring and
control solutions deliver proven results to over 16,000 customers across the globe. Its customers are
supported by the largest industrial software ecosystem, including 4,200 partners and 5,700 certified
developers. AVEVA is headquartered in Cambridge, UK, with over 4,400 employees at 80 locations in
over 40 countries.

Data + AI in Power

© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Reliability-centered maintenance

Prevent Equipment Failures

•

•

Predictive monitoring of industrial equipment assets
{Compressors, Pumps, Gearbox’s, Motors, Turbines, Boilers, Generators, etc.}

Early warning detection and diagnosis of equipment problems

Benefits

•

Improve asset reliability and performance

• Reduce energy consumption and lost power

• Reduce maintenance costs

• Optimize maintenance planning

• Achieve fast time to value through out-of-the-box predictive monitoring of

industrial assets

© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Risk-
Based Maintenance

Predictive
Maintenance

Condition-Based Maintenance

Preventive Maintenance

Reactive Maintenance

Integrated AI
Predictive + Prescriptive + Prognostic

© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Historical

Real-Time

What Happened

What’s Happening

Historical Domain

Real-Time Domain

Prescriptive

Prognostics

What to do

How bad will it get

Maintenance Action Domain

Artificial Intelligence Domain

Predictive

What If

Machine Learning Domain

Predictive Analytics: Assets

Observations

Received Predictive notification due to Combustion Turbine blade path
temperature spread increasing

Spread increase was caused by early progression of a transition piece failure

Slow propagation before failure would have caused large amounts of wasted
fuel and increased GHG emissions

Customer calculated over $4 million in avoided costs

© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Predictive Analytics: Operations

© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Observations

Received Predictive notification on low extraction steam temperature

Could have gone weeks before the plant found the issue

Significant financial impact

Significant environmental impacts:

Excess Fuel Usage -> Increased Emissions

Lower Heat Rate due to low extraction steam temperature requires
increased fuel consumption, thus increased emissions

Savings: (approx.) 2,000 metric tons of CO2 emissions averted per week

Water Usage Waste

Steam attemperation requires significant water

Savings: (approx.) 100,000 gallons of Demineralized Water

Predictive Analytics: Green Energy

Observations

Several wind turbines were identified having bearing vibrations too
high for the level of power generated.

Rollers cocked 180 degrees were found with failed retainers.

1 maintenance trip required to correct all issues.

Early detection avoided significant damage to the turbines.

Early catches like this help improve the cost effectiveness and
reliability of renewable energy.

2.43 failures per wind turbine per year*

© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

* WMEP database (1,500 turbines, 17 years, and 64,000 reports)

Now integrate Simulation

© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Determining the best action when an anomaly is detected

Balancing Risk Vs Cost

Stop and fix it now?

Wait until planned shutdown?

Can I operate differently?

• Fix immediately to eliminate risk of

• Risk of equipment failure during

failure

operations

• Return to standard operations quicker

• If no failure, then less cost (…maybe)

• A change in configuration could reduce

the risk of failure while extending the life
of the equipment at risk

© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA Predictive Asset Optimization

Heat Exchanger Fouling

Gas Turbine Routine Maintenance Optimization

•

•

•

RULE allows us to forecast
remaining run time to hit
operational limits. We will hit a
safe operating limit for tube skin
metal temperature within two
weeks.

HDR models track changes in heat
transfer characteristics which allow
us to detect and monitor fouling. In
February 2020 fouling has become
worse after a water contamination
event.

Impact and Optimization shows that
by a 10% reduction in duct burner
firing we can generate enough
headroom in tube skin temperatures
to continue operation and avoid an
immediate forced outage during
which time we can continue to
monitor for further degradation.

•

HDR models track
cumulative cost of gas
turbine filter fouling and
payback-time for
replacement of each
filter section enabling
Optimization of
maintenance intervals /
reduction of unnecessary
expenditure.

© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

•

HDR models track
changes in KPIs
corrected for load and
ambient temperature:

Power Output

Heat Rate

Compressor Efficiency

Compressor Pressure Ratio

Turbine Efficiency

Exhaust Temperature

linkedin.com/company/aveva

@avevagroup

ABOUT AVEVA

AVEVA is a world leader in industrial software, providing engineering and operational solutions across multiple industries,
including oil and gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power and utilities, marine, renewables, and food and beverage. Our
agnostic and open architecture helps organizations design, build, operate, maintain and optimize the complete lifecycle
of complex industrial assets, from production plants and offshore platforms to manufactured consumer goods.

Over 20,000 enterprises in over 100 countries rely on AVEVA to help them deliver life’s essentials: safe and reliable
energy, food, medicines, infrastructure and more. By connecting people with trusted information and AI -enriched
insights, AVEVA enables teams to engineer efficiently and optimize operations, driving growth and sustainability.

Named as one of the world’s most innovative companies, AVEVA supports customers with open solutions and the
expertise of more than 6,400 employees, 5,000 partners and 5,700 certified developers. The company is headquartered
in Cambridge, UK.

Learn more at www.aveva.com

www.aveva.com

© 2025 AVEVA Group Limited or its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.




# SOURCE: vendor_aveva\ISM User Interface Rev 1.2.pdf
---

ISM User Interface

Melinda Hemstead, Data Consultant

April 2019 | Version 1.2

User Interface

Example Class Library

The user interface opens and displays your current
catalogue folders, which version of ISM you are
connected to and your User name

ISM – Help / About

Help: Information on wording, symbols, icons,
colours, validation etc. can be found and accessed
from the        icon in the top right hand side of the ISM
screen.

About: This area shows the ISM information, plug-in
elements that have been enabled, the available
readers (how you can load ISM), exporters (different
export formats available) and validators

Folders

Folders shown are those that are automatically
loaded into ISM.

Any Standard you add into ISM will be stored
under the ‘Personal’ folder.

You can move your Standards to different folders
simply by dragging and dropping them into a new
folder. They highlight yellow when being moved.

You can create new folders by right clicking on the
Catalogue area and adding a new ‘root folder’

ISM Content Discovery Sidebar

Example Class Library

Catalogue Details

Example Class Library

The eye icon       reveals information on your
Class Library:
Details – This is the information you loaded
into your Class Library header tab.
Composition – You can add Libraries or part
Libraries here into your main Class Library to
become part of a composite Class Library
Approved By – The approvers information will
be located in this section once your Class
Library is approved
Extension Namespaces – This area shows all
the extension namespaces you have added
into your Class Library.
Extensions – This is an expandable section
which shows the actual extensions you have
used.
Class Library Policy Configuration – This area
shows the ISM Validation Rules that would be
applied to your Class Library at validation
(configurable).
Naming Conventions – If you have added
Naming Conventions to your Details tab they
would reflect in this area

Standard and Change Actions

Example Class Library

Unlocked

Example Class Library

Locked

The locked screens display more functionality than the
unlocked screen.
When ISM is locked you can perform additions, modifications
and validation to your Class Library

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Catalogue

Example Class Library
Example Class Library

The bars icon          closes the Catalogue area and opens
your Class Library to the full ISM screen

This makes the reading pane larger and easier to review

Search

Search – you can configure your search, to locate by
Id, Name, Description, Aspect or multiple areas by
checking the boxes.
Filters can also be applied to a search – Concept Filter
and Extension Filters for filtering search of loaded CL.
Other filters can be applied when you have added
other languages into your Class Library.
Modification and Validation filters only used when
you have produced a Compare or Merge or ran the
Validation over your Class Library.

Browse

Browse – this contains all the main function areas of
ISM.

Favourites

Favourites – if you add any of your Classes, Attributes,
UoM etc. to be your favourites, they will automatically
be added to this area.

You can ‘add to favourites’ in 2 areas – Browse and
Content areas

Open Objects

You can open many objects in the main viewing area at
any one time. Open objects display along the top of
the interface and will remain open until you close them
(via the ‘x’ next to the object name)
You can switch from one object to another easily.
When you have many objects open you will see a
down arrow, where you can quickly close down all
open objects with one click.

Create a Load Template

Creating a new blank load template.
Click on the +
Add in a unique Id (Id’s cannot be
duplicated in ISM), Name and
Description for your Class
Library/Standard.

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Import and Save

Import your Class Library/Standard into ISM

Example Class Library

Save your Class Library/Standard into ISM with a
unique Id

Example Class Library

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Editing

Example Class Library

Lock to edit your Class Library

Example Class Library

Editing tools revealed

Example Class Library

If you make an update to
information within your
Class Library the updated
information turns yellow
with ISM automatically
refreshing the screen.

A pencil icon will also be
displayed showing that you
are editing your Class
Library

New icons also become
available to accept or
reject your update

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Merging Information into your Class Library/Standard

Example Class Library

To Merge another Class
Library/Standard into your
existing Class Library, click the
Import icon (when you have
locked your CL the import icon
becomes ‘merge’) and direct
ISM to your load template
Note: all updates or new Class
Libraries are required to be in
the ISM load template.

Clearing your Class Library/Standard While your Class Library/Standard is locked you also

have the capability to empty the contents to load new
information – be aware that this will clear all the
contents, but preserve the columns/namespaces etc.

Example Class Library

To accept the changes click

To cancel your clearance click

Example Class Library

Export

Example Class Library

Export your Class Library, choosing which
format you require

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Validation

Example Class Library

Click on the icon and select your chosen Validators
from the dropdown menu. Click on the Validate
button to commence validation

Close your Class Library/Standard

Example Class Library

To close your Class Library/Standard click on the  
and this takes you to the main menu for your
imported Class Libraries/Standards

To go back to the main menu when merging or
comparing Class Libraries use the  <   as this leaves
your Class Library active

Compare

Go back to the main menu using the       to leave your Class
Library active (shown in previous slide)

Your active Class Library is now shown by the         symbol.

Choose the Class Library you’d like to Compare it with or
Merge into your active Class Library. Click on the down
symbol        to reveal the available options.
Click on the required option and the process will begin

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

linkedin.com/company/aveva

@avevagroup

ABOUT AVEVA
AVEVA is a global leader in engineering and industrial software driving digital transformation across
the entire asset and operational life cycle of capital-intensive industries.

The company’s engineering, planning and operations, asset performance, and monitoring and
control solutions deliver proven results to over 16,000 customers across the globe. Its customers are
supported by the largest industrial software ecosystem, including 4,200 partners and 5,700 certified
developers. AVEVA is headquartered in Cambridge, UK, with over 4,400 employees at 80 locations in
over 40 countries.
aveva.com

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 7

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Welcome to Information Standards Manager

Welcome to Information Standards
Manager

AVEVA Information Standards Manager is an information standards authoring, management, and governance
solution - available on CONNECT and on-prem.

This guide describes everything you need relating to creating, mapping and managing your industrial information
standard, and thus achieving greater control over critical industrial information on projects and in operations.

Page 8

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Welcome to Information Standards Manager

Technical Support and Training Information

Technical Support

If you encounter software issues or have suggestions for software improvements, contact the AVEVA Helpdesk
(https://softwaresupport.aveva.com), and register a support incident.

After you register a support incident, we know your identity (from your customer ID) and the details of the
incident, we endeavor to fix the software issue or consider the software improvement suggestion as soon as
possible, and notify you about the status of the incident.

If you are an accredited user of the AVEVA products, you are entitled to use the AVEVA Helpdesk, for which you
should have valid login credentials. For any other queries, contact the nearest AVEVA Regional Support Center
(https://www.aveva.com/en/support).

Training Information

For information about product training courses, see the Product Training section of AVEVA's website
(https://www.aveva.com/en/training), or contact the nearest AVEVA Regional Support Center
(https://www.aveva.com/en/support).

Page 9

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Product Overview

Product Overview

Understanding and managing the rules about industrial information is the first step the process of creating and
maintaining an accurate Digital Twin. The AVEVA Information Standards Manager (henceforth, referred to as the
ISM) delivers trust in the industrial twin by setting baseline quality assurance rules for information compliance,
consistency, and completeness and provides the data model mapping rules that enable the harmonization of
information held in multiple applications.

ISM can be easily accessed via CONNECT, AVEVA’s flagship cloud platform. Alternatively, it can be installed on
premises.

Key Features of ISM:

•  Create, edit and govern asset class libraries and information standards.

•  Use industry/corporate/site layers to model industrial information.

•  Manage the mapping of industry, domain and software data models.

•  Track changes to information standards - assess impact of a change and view audit history.

•  Report on errors and inconsistencies in your information standards.

•  Present class libraries in different languages to suit the needs of a multilingual workforce/clientele.

•  Define asset information lifecycle rules.

•  Clearly communicate information requirements with all stakeholders.

•  Provide validated configurations to authoring software.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Statement of Withdrawal

The following technologies are no longer supported:

•  Microsoft® Windows® 8.1 Professional (64-bit)

•  Microsoft® Windows Server® 2016 (64-bit)

•  Microsoft® SQL Server® 2017 (64- bit)

•  Microsoft® SQL Server® 2016 (64-bit)

Product Quality Statement

The development, maintenance and testing of AVEVA Solutions’ software products is carried out in accordance
with the AVEVA Quality Management System lifecycle processes and this document includes a summary of the
testing carried out on this release and a list of significant defects known to exist at the time of release.

Development Testing

Developers have carried out unit testing of each enhancement and/or fix in the development environment to
verify that any new functionalities have been correctly implemented and identified defects have been corrected.

Regression tests have been run in the development environment to verify that enhancements and/or fixes have
not adversely affected the integrity of the product.

System Testing

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Get Started with ISM

Get Started with ISM

This chapter introduces you to AVEVA Information Standards Manager. Specifically, this chapter provides
information on the following topics:

1.  Logging on to AVEVA Information Standards Manager

2.  Prerequisites

3.  Accessing the Home Page

4.  Understanding menus and basic page components (User Interface elements)

5.  Key concepts

6.  User roles

Prerequisites

Assumptions

This document assumes that AVEVA Information Standards Manager is installed and deployed using the
instructions provided in AVEVA Information Standards Manager Installation Guide, and that you use AVEVA
Information Standards Manager in accordance with the requirements, procedures and policies of your company.

Prerequisites

For using AVEVA Information Standards Manager, ensure that your account is created by the System
Administrator and you are granted the required permissions.

The User Interface

The user interface consists of the following:

•  The left side of the blue bar is used for Catalogues. You can use these Catalogues to manage Users and

Standards. Whilst in the Catalogue view, you can select, import or create Standards. When no Standards are
selected you get a tree view of folders that contain Compositions and Standards. If you select a Standard, the
default view is of the active Standard and its audit trail. For understanding Catalogues, see Managing the
Catalogue. For understanding how to manage users, see Managing Users and User Groups.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 27

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Get Started with ISM

•  The blue bar is for Content Discovery (for details, see Content Discovery Sidebar). The right side of the blue

bar is for content. The content area is a tabbed panel where each tab displays details for a given entity in the
Standard. The Browse section provides accessibility to the underlying functions, such as Classes, Attributes,
Units of Measure and Taxonomy, depending on what you select from the Content Discovery Sidebar.

•  The browsing area is for navigation and use. The details on icons and icon annotations. Use this area for
creating and managing Class Libraries, Attributes, Units of Measure, searching, and maintaining lists of
favorite items.

•  A global toolbar is displayed at the top of the content area. It is reserved for the following information:

•  Default Favorite List: Provides the default list of objects marked as favorites. To create a list of favorites,

or edit an existing list, see Use Favorite Lists.

•  Standards Details: Provides the details of the Standard currently locked by you. Alternatively, you may

click the Details button (

) on Catalogue. See Viewing and Configuring Standards Details.

•  Logged-in User: Displays the current user's name.

•  Information and Help (

): Provides the summary of the user guide, such as key concepts, UI, audit trail,

icon, icon annotations and grid.

•  The Content area is where you will manage your data.

Content Discovery Sidebar

The Content Discovery sidebar provides you the access to the following capabilities. Use this sidebar to create
Standards, use Favorites List, implement Configuration Schema or use the Search:

operating on a Standard or

Key Concepts

Descriptions for common words used in the AVEVA Information Standards Manager vocabulary:

Word

Abstract

Aspect

Description

An Abstract class is the base definition for more
specialized classes. For example, Instrument
Equipment is an abstract and a parent class for Alarm
Panel. In an authoring or managing tool, they would
have instantiated objects.

A semantic categorization that can be used to create
rules that help process and populate similar or linked
values.

Class Concept

Identifies the purpose of a Class type:

•  Functional Class Concept identifies that the Class

relates to the design work.

•  Physical Class Concept identifies that the Class

relates to the Assets.

•  Document Class Concept identifies that the Class

relates to Documents.

•  General Class Concept identifies that the Class

relates to everything else.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Word

Class Library

Composition

Concept

Derived

Discipline

Entity

Extends/Extension

Hierarchy

Language

Namespace

Nodes

Nomenclature

Permissible

Standard

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Get Started with ISM

Description

A collection of Standards constituting a holistic
definition of Classes, Attributes and related
information.

A collection of independent Standards that make up a
Class Library.

Identifies whether an object is permissible on a
specific Class Concept.

An attribute can relate to, or may be permissible on, a
Functional Class as well as a Physical Class.

The entity or value is inherited from a parent in the
entity hierarchy.

A specialized team, which has the responsibility for
the identified item. Different teams may have
responsibility for the item over its life cycle.

Any item in the Standard that can have an identifier
designated. For example, Functional Classes, Physical
Classes, Document Classes, Attributes, Enumerated
Lists and UoM Unit.

Additional user-defined metadata on an entity.

A structured order of information and properties in a
system where the allocated properties are inherited
by its child items.

Help us to define and identify the same item in
different languages used on a Project or Asset. For
example, English and Norwegian.

A shareable class of unique elements. For example, a
set of symbols or file types.

A point within a structured order of information.

AVEVA Information Standards Manager uses standard
terminology. However, the use of terminology could
differ based on standards. It helps map the
terminologies use across different standards. For
example, CFIHOS and ISO.

An entity being associated with another entity.

A defined procedure or level of work mandated by

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Get Started with ISM

Word

Description

project company or standards body.

User Roles

To manage individual users access to functions, features and tasks in the application, the user privileges are
organized by user Roles. A Role is a set of permissions that are granted to a user. It controls the access to
application modules and the work flow. A user can have only one active Role, which can only be administered
and managed by the Tool Administrator. Each Role determines the authorization granted to a user to perform
specific operations. All users that have a specific Role assigned inherit the privileges for that Role.

A Role with higher capacity will include the privileges for all other lesser capacity Roles. For example, if a user has
the Administrator Role, they enjoy all privileges for the Creator and the Administrator Roles. Any user
permissions granted on an individual folder or Standard will be restricted by the role the user currently has. This
means that a Read-only user will not be able to be assign any edit permissions. The Role cannot be amended
through the UI. For more information, refer to AVEVA Information Standards Manager Installation Guide.

The following table provides a summary of Roles in AVEVA Information Standards Manager:

Role Name

Administrator

Creator

Author

Reader

Color Coding

Responsibility

Only a user with Administrator Role can unlock a
Standard that may be locked by another user for
editing or permanently delete an Obsolete Standard.
This enables an additional layer of security. Before the
Administrator unlocks the Standard, they must first
save it and tag it appropriately before making further
changes.

Creates and edits Standards and the Catalogue
folders. Can make a Standard Obsolete. Cannot
permanently delete a Standard.

Edits Standards, sets Class hierarchies. Cannot create a
Standard.

Does not have edit permissions. Can only view the
information.

entity or value. The following lists the backgrounds and their meanings:

Color

The entity or value has been derived from elsewhere.

Input Field Borders

Borders on input fields are used to convey information on whether the contained value is editable.

Border

Description

A solid border means that the value is editable.

A dashed border means that the value is read-only.

Grids

Grids display tabular data, for example, a list of permissible attributes for a class. The following is an example of a
grid:

All grids have some common features, such as:

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 32

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Get Started with ISM

•  Sorting: By default, most grids are sorted by the sort order and name. Change this sorting by clicking on the

header. The first click sorts in the ascending order. The second, in the descending order. The third click
reverts the list to the default.

•  Selecting Columns: Right-click a column header to choose a columns to display.

•  Paging: Grids display limited entities per page. If the number of items in a grid cannot fit a single page, the
number of pages will appear at the bottom-center of the grid. Use arrows keys to navigate between pages.

•  Item count: On the task bar of the grid, you will find information on the number of items in the grid and

what items are displayed on the current page.

•  Adding items: Some grids support adding of items. In such grids, look for the Add (

) button at the

bottom left of the grid.

•  Grouping: Some grids contain grouped data. Grouping is predefined in a grid and it is not possible to group
by an arbitrary field. Groups have precedence over sorting and any sorting applied will sort items in the
groups.

•  Reorder: Drag columns in the desired order.

Tokens

A token is an annotated link to an entity. It represents the actual data that lies elsewhere in the application. The
following icon is an example of a token for the functional class Agitator. Click the icon to access the details
contained with the actual entity:

Tokens are annotated with colours. The background colour of the entity icon is determined by the linked entity's
modification state. For example, if the entity is modified the icon appears yellow. The following is a token that
has its entity modified, while its name is derived from a parent:

For information on colours, see Color Coding.

Click the User Groups icon (
Manager. Users who have access to AVEVA Information Standards Manager are listed under the Everyone user

Add User Groups

To create a user group in AVEVA Information Standards Manager:

1.  Click

.

2.  Enter the name of the user group.

3.  Right-click the group you added, and select Add Users to Group.

4.

In the Add Users to Group window, choose the required user from the drop-down menu.

5.  Click Select.

Remove Users from Groups

To remove a user from a group:

1.  Expand a group.

2.  Right-click the name of the user not required in the group.

3.  Select Remove from Group.

Delete User Groups

Manage User Group Permissions

You can only assign and manage properties on groups. Right-click and select Properties to view the default group
settings.

To assign and manage properties for users:

1.  Select Everyone in the Permissions section. Otherwise, to view and assign permissions for a user, select the

required user from the section.

2.

In the Permissions section, select or clear the Allow or Deny check boxes that correspond to the following
capabilities to permit or restrict the user accordingly: Read, Modify, Delete, Manage Permissions, Add Items
and Remove Items.

3.  Click Save.

Manage the Catalogue

The Catalogue area stages folders that contain Compositions and Standards. There are four folders available out
of the box. But, you can create a root folder, if required.

As part of the standard installation of AVEVA Information Standards Manager, the following four folders are
available:

•  Frequently Used: Shortcuts for those Standards that you use frequently are displayed here. To perform tasks

on a Standard, right-click it within its parent folder, and not within the Frequently Used folder.

•  Undesignated: Standards that currently do not have any accessibility shortcuts are placed in this folder and
are visible to all users if they have required permissions. For information, see Permissions for Descendants.

•  Personal: Content is only visible to other users if they have permissions. You can delete a Standard placed

within this folder, unless you have assigned permissions for other users to view and edit that Standard. If you
have tagged a Standard in the Personal folder, and permitted other users to access or use the Standard, you
cannot delete it even if you have the Creator Role.

•  Public: Shared by all users. All Standards in the Public folder, irrespective of the underlying access

permissions, are visible to all users.

In addition to the default folders that are mentioned above, you can create a new folder and drag the required
Standard into it to assign access permissions.

To add a root folder:

1.  Right-click the empty area, and select Add Root Folder.

2.  Enter a name for the folder and click Add.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Work with Standards

The Catalogue enables you to import, select and create Standards. You are presented with two views: Standards
and Active Standard. The Standards view contains a list of Standards, and the Active Standard view contains an
audit trail.

•  Standards list actions:

•  Open: Opens the Standard

•  Audit Trail: Shows the audit trail of the Standard without opening it

•  Audit trail commit actions:

•  Open: Opens the Change Set for viewing.

•  Compare with Active Class Library: Compares the Change Set against Active Standard/Change Set.

Creates a temporary Standard.

•  Merge into Active Class Library: Merges the content of the commit into the Active Standard.

Note: You can access the Audit Trail for the latest 200 changes.

When you either create or import a Standard using the global toolbar, it appears in the Personal folder. As long
as you do not either tag a Standard in the folder or give other users permissions to access or use to it, no other
user can view or use the Standard. You will be the only user (other than the Administrator) who has permissions
to permanently delete it in such a case.

Import Standards

Important: Ensure that the size of the imported file is less than 10 MB. For files larger than 10 MB, reduce the
file size using a file compressor tool, such as WinZip, before uploading.

To import a Standard:

1.  Click the Import (

) button. You can also right-click a folder and select Import from File.

2.  Click Choose File and select the required document.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 49

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

3.  Select from the options:

•  Import as a new Standard: This creates a new Standard in the Personal folder.

•  Merge into the Active Standard: Ensure that the Standard is locked for editing before selecting this option.

•  Merge into the copy of the active Standard

1.  Click the Save As (

) button.

2.  Create a new ID for your Standard and optionally enter a name and description in the respective fields.

Note: If the Name field is left blank, ID is displayed instead of Name in the user interface.

3.  Click Save As.

4.  Click the Lock for Editing (

) button to lock the Standard to make changes.

Note: You must have the Creator Role to create or import a Standard. If you have the Author Role, you
can only edit an existing standard. For information, see User Roles.

5.  After you have completed the changes, click the Validate (

) button to accept the changes and to

validate the Standard. For information, see Validating Standards.

6.  Click either the Unlock and Save Changes (

) button to commit the changes and unlock the Standard

to make it available to everyone for use or click the Unlock and Discard Changes (
the changes. Optionally, tag a release.

) button to discard

View and Configure Standard Details

Clicking the Details button (

) on Catalogue enables you to perform the following actions:

•  Viewing Standards details

•  Viewing approval history

•  Creating or managing Compositions

•  Managing extensions

•  Managing validation rules

•  Implementing corresponding identifiers

View Standards Details

To navigate through the metadata of the currently selected Standard:

1.

In the Details tab, view the required information:

a.  The unique identification for the Standard. This supports alphanumeric characters, and does not auto-

increment.

b.  The Name in this is an optional field. If left blank, ID is displayed instead of Name in the user interface.

This field accepts alphanumeric characters. This field is editable.

c.  The description of what the Standard contains. This field is editable.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 50

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

2.  Select the Abstract check box if the currently selected Standard only contains abstract Classes.

3.  The Version field is read-only and displays the current version number. It auto-populates with the next four-

digit version number when you check out the Standard again.

4.  The Version Date (UTC) registers the date of change. This field is read-only.

5.  The Locked for editing by field displays the user who has currently locked the Standard for editing.

6.  The Sort Order field displays the order in which the Standard is sorted within a folder or composition.

View Approval History

The fields on the Approved By tab display information after you perform Validation of the currently Active
Standard. The Name field populates the approver. The Version field populates the approved version number. The
Description field contains the details of the validation. For information on validation, see Validating Standards.

Create or Manage Compositions

Use the fields in this tab to add items to a Composition. If you have read-only privileges, you cannot modify the
information in this tab. However, you can reorder the records displayed in a Composition. The Standards take
precedence based on their order in the composition.

Notes:

•  You may work on a Class Library, where the Standard contained within the Composition is locked and

changed by another user. Such changes do not reflect on your Class Library until the Standard is saved and
unlocked by the other user and you have refreshed your web browser.

•  You may lock a Class Library for editing on a web browser, while working on a Standard contained within its
Composition on a different web browser. In such cases, the changes you make to the Standard may not be
reflected in the Class Library until you reload the Standard in the web browser you used to access the Class
Library.

To add a Composition:

1.  On the Composition tab, click the Add button. From the dropdown in the Add Items to the Composition

window, select the required Catalogue Standard.

Note: Catalogue Standards previously added to the Standard do not display in the dropdown.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 51

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

2.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close.

3.  The ID and Name fields on the Composition tab will display the added record.

4.  The Required Version field displays the version required by the application. You can use this field to set the
version of the Standard to use. The version histories of changes saved and tagged by you are listed in the
dropdown for this field. You can have this value set to a version which may be different from the value in the
Actual Version field, which is read-only.

5.  Click Close.

Nomenclature mapping using composition

Example:

•  Let us assume we have two standards (A and B). Standard A is applied as a composition into Standard B.

When we are adding or modifying composition (Nomenclature) items into Standard B, it means we are doing
this operation into the current Standard B. In this case, any item which we were modifying is treated as a
new item in current Standard B, because earlier that item was not actually part of Standard B (it was
inherited from Standard A). Hence, it shows as a new item. Refer to the screenshot below for more details.

•  Before adding a new identifier into Standard B.

•  After adding new identifier into Standard B, it is treated as a new item. Hence, it is displaying as Green. After

saving it will change to white.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 52

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

•  We can identify whether the value is derived or not on right clicking the respective text box as shown in the

following screenshot. Standard B can be exported to find the derived value as well.

•  The following screenshot shows the Accumulator item is added as a new item along with the two identifiers

(FC-0078 and FC-0079) after saving Standard B.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 53

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

Note: After performing the above steps, any changes made to Standard A should not reflect into Standard B.
Application behaves the same for "Import and merge option" or "Edit manually" into current standard.

Manage Extensions

Extensions enable you to add metadata to an object. This can include configuration maps that help configure the
same value across multiple tools. Right-click the parent, transitional folder to add extensions.

Before you manage extensions ensure that you have the required top-level extensions defined in the Extension
Namespaces tab of Standard Details. Here you can define properties with namespaces.

Defining Top-level Extension Namespaces

To define top-level extension namespaces:

1.  On the Extension Namespaces tab, click the Add button to create an extension namespace.

2.

In the Prefix field, enter a word of your choice. Ensure that the prefix name does not contain a colon (:) or
space, and does not start with a number.

3.  Enter a name for your extension namespace in the Name field.

4.  Use the URI field to input a universal resource identifier (URI), which is a universally unique string.

5.  Optionally, in the Description field, provide a purposeful description of what the extension namespace does.
You may have applied the extension namespace to Attributes, Standards, Enumerations or a Functional Class.

After defining top-level extension namespaces, you can define object-specific extensions.

The Extensions tab on Standards Details is reserved for managing extensions for other applications, such as
AVEVA Asset Information Management. For information, refer to the AVEVA Information Standards Manager
Importing from MS Excel document.

Adding a Namespace

To add an object-level namespace:

1.  Right-click a parent to add a namespace.

2.

In the Add Extension Namespace Node window, select the required namespace from the dropdown.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 54

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

Using Transitional Folder

To create a transitional folder:

1.  To add an extension node, click Add Node.

2.  Enter a unique identification and name. The Name is a mandatory field.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close.

Adding a Property

To extend a property:

1.  Right-click Add Property to extend properties.

2.  Enter a unique property name, which is mandatory, along with its value in the respective fields. For example,

GroupSuffix.

3.  Select the Make Derivable check box, if required. In such a case, the new object will derive properties from

its parent.

4.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 55

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

Tag a Release

To ensure that only the latest and updated Standard is used by others, you can tag a released Standard. When
you tag a saved Standard as Released, you increment the major and minor release numbers. When you save a
Standard, you increment the dotted release numbers for a major release.

To tag a validated Standard as Released, use the following procedure:

1.  Go to an Active Standard.

2.  Ensure that you do not have the Standard locked for editing. This will create a four digit revision number.

Alternatively, click Unlock and Discard Changes.

Note: An unreleased Standard cannot be tagged.

3.  Click the Tag as Release (

) button.

4.  Enter the Major, Minor and Revision numbers, respectively. Ensure that you select an overall value higher

than that of the current release or version.

5.  Click Release.

Note: As indicated in the screenshot, it is recommended that you validate a Standard before tagging it for
release. For information, see Validating Standards.

Use Classes

A Class is an object that identifies the requirements of data attributes or elements with their relationships and
interpretations applied. By establishing Class hierarchies, you can define and control the behaviour of the

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 56

underlying instantiated objects.

Introduce Class Types

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

There are four Class types, Functional (
), that define the
nature of the data they contain, respectively. Each Class type must be categorised according to the details it
holds. For information, see Class Concepts in Key Concepts.

) and General (

), Document (

), Physical (

Create Classes

To create a class or sub-class, ensure that you have the Creator privilege and have the Standard locked for
editing:

1.  Right-click a Class Concept folder and select Add Class or Add Sub-Class.

2.  Enter the required information:

a.  Enter a unique identification for the class. This supports alphanumeric characters. This field does not

auto-increment.

Note: Whilst the application accepts white spaces and special characters, it is not recommended to use
them.

b.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If left blank, ID is displayed instead of Name in

the user interface. This field accepts alphanumeric characters.

c.  Describe what the Class does for you.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This

brings you to the Details tab of that Class.

4.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

5.  Select the Abstract check box if the Class is generic and will include more specific Classes.

6.  Define the Discipline for the Class. This is a text box unless the appropriate Aspect has been associated to an
Enumerated List. For information, see Understanding Aspects. Otherwise, this displays as a drop-down
menu. For information on selecting a discipline from the dropdown menu, see Using Disciplines.

7.  Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in which Classes are listed under their

parent folder in the tree view.

The Class is defined.

Notes:

•  To discontinue using a Class, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties section on the Details

tab.

•  For details on adding and managing taxonomies in the Taxonomy Occurrences tab, see Maintaining

Taxonomies.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Change the Parent Class

You can extend from only one Parent Class. To change an extension from a Parent Class:

1.

In the Class that you wish to move from one parent to another, go to the Details tab.

2.  Click the Plus button (

) and select the required Class from the dropdown.

3.

In the Select Parent Class window, choose the required Parent Class from the dropdown.

4.  Click Select.

Allocate Attributes to Classes

There are two ways in which you can apply an attribute to a Class. You can manually allocate an attribute to a
Class, which is described below, or a class can inherit all the attributes allocated to its parent class. The inherited
Classes contain a green inward arrow sign on the icon. See Icon Annotations and the term Hierarchy in Key
Concepts for information.

To allocate an attribute to a Class, ensure that you have the Creator or Author privilege and have the Standard
locked for editing:

1.

In a Class that you want to allocate an Attribute, navigate to the Permissible Attributes tab.

2.  Click the Add button (

).

3.

In the Add Permissible Attributes window, select the required attribute from the dropdown.

4.  To create the record and continue to create records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close to add the

record and close the window.

Explore the Network

The Network tab is a visual representation of the relationships between objects. Use this tab to drill down to the
point where data is displayed in rounded rectangles, which are data objects. You may click these objects to open
a network of their own in a separate tab.

For the top-level visual representation, click the Network View (
) button to
view the the tabular format. The buttons appear as you hover over a parent object. The List View (also called the
Grid View) and Network View buttons are displayed in the right-click menu on only the top-level item in the tree
view for Classes and Attributes.

) button or use the List View (

Applying Extensions

In the Extensions tab, you can add and manage extensions to enable mapping with third-party tools. Expand

Adding Language Translations

displays the available language translations. For information, see Adding a Language Translation section.

Work with Attributes

An Attribute is a value the user of an authoring tool defines. For example, Name, Design Pressure or Length (as
opposed to a property, which is an intrinsic value of the data object itself and may also be referred to as
metadata). When you extend a Class all its attributes are inherited by its target and its child classes. Such
attribute values can be overridden if required. It is recommended that the attribute identifier value does not
contain white space.

When associating an Attribute to a Class, the Attribute can have properties based on the instantiation. For
example, the Number may carry a different label of Name on two different Classes. That is, on a Physical Class it
will be Asset Number and on a Document Class it will be Document Number.

Define Permissible Attributes

Before creating a permissible attribute, ensure that you have the Author privilege and have the Standard locked
for editing:

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 61

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

1.  You can create an Attribute at any level by right-clicking and selecting Add Attribute. You can also create an

attribute within a folder or group.

2.  Enter the required information:

a.  Enter a unique identification for the attribute. This supports alphanumeric characters. This field does not

auto-increment.

Note: Whilst the application accepts white spaces and special characters, it is not recommended to use
them.

b.  Enter a user-readable name for the identification in this optional field. If left blank, ID is displayed instead

of Name in the user interface. This field accepts alphanumeric characters.

c.  Describe the purpose of the attribute.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This

brings you to the Details tab of that Attribute.

4.

5.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

In the Concept field, select all the concepts (Class Types) that this attribute may be used on. If you do not
select a specific concept, you will not be able to find the attribute when searching for it from a Class of the
unselected concept.

6.  Select a Group. This helps create a folder structure for similar Attributes.

7.  Select a Discipline. Depending on whether the appropriate Aspect is applied to an Enumerated List Object,
the selection for the fields appear as either a dropdown or a text field. For information on selecting a
discipline from the dropdown menu, see Using Disciplines.

8.  Select from a list of Data Types that define the Attribute. See the note below for a list of data types.

9.  Enter the acceptable number of characters or value in the Size field. For example, if you enter 300 in Size and

select String as your Data Type, the value can contain up to 300 characters.

10.  Select the UoM Required check box if the unit of measure is required for the Attribute. Typically, a unit of

measure may be required for Data Types, such as Integer or Decimal.

11.  Select the required Unit of Measure Class. For information, see Creating and Defining UoM Class.

12.  Select from the Presence dropdown.

13.  Select the Validation Type for the Attribute. Based on your selection for the field, the Validation Rule field

will vary as follows:

•  When you select Enumeration as the Validation Type, the Validation Rule will be set to Enumeration List.

It is here that you can associate an Enumerated List with an Attribute.

•  When you select Value Range Inclusive, the Validation Rule will be set to Maximum Value and Minimum

Value.

•  When you select Regular Expression as the Validation Type, the Validation Rule will ask you to provide

the Regular Expression.

•  When you select Association as the Validation Type, the Validation Rule will ask you to set the

Association Rule and the Target associated with the rule.

•  If no Validation Type is required, you can set the type as <unselect>.

14.  Use the Min Occurs and Max Occurs fields, respectively, to specify the minimum and maximum number of

values to be provided for the Attribute when loading data from other applications, such as AVEVA
Gatekeeper.

15.  Select a Maturity Level for the Attribute. For information, see Defining Maturity Levels.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 62

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

16.  Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in which all Attributes will be listed in a

folder in the tree view.

17.  Enter an Aspect if you have an enumerated list associated with the Attribute. This enables you to select from

a fixed set of possible values or associations for the Attribute.

The permissible attribute is now defined.

Note:

•  To discontinue using an Attribute, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties section on the

Details tab.

•  The following is a list of the data types used in ISM, along with how they appear in the ISM UI and in

exported files.

Data Type in ISM UI

Boolean

Integer

Decimal

Date and Time

Date

Time

Duration

Day

Uri

Base64 Binary

String

Boolean

Decimal

DateTime

Create Permissible Associations

An Attribute can be used to define an association between a Target Class or Classes and the Class that the

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 63

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

attribute is associated to. If you are associating to multiple Classes, you are restricted to the same Class Concept,
meaning that, you can have a single association to both a Document and a General Class. This type of an
attribute is referred to as permissible association rather than as permissible attribute.

The association can only be defined in one direction. However, the reverse can be done using appropriate
extensions, where required, such as in AVEVA Asset Information Management.

To create an attribute as a permissible association, first define a permissible attribute and then, use the following
procedure:

1.

In the Details tab, go to the Properties Specific to Attribute section.

2.

In the Validation Type field, select Association.

3.  The Validation Rule field is replaced with Association Type and Valid Target Class fields. In the Association

Type field, enter the association condition, such as Fulfilled By.

4.  The filter criteria can be toggled to show either only selected data or all values, which populates the

corresponding descendants as well.

5.

In the Target Class Field, search and select the target Class or Classes.

Note: You can select multiple Classes as long as they are from the same Concept.

6.  Click Add and Close.

Manage Units of Measure

Unit of measure is applicable to only Attributes. A Unit of Measure or Unit of Measurement (UoM) is used for
measuring the same kind of quantity. For example, millimeter and kilometer are measuring units, while length is
a collection of such units, and hence is called a Unit of Measure Class.

Measure Class:

A Measure Class is a collection of Units of Measure, which can be associated to an Attribute.

Measure Unit:

A Measure Unit is a measurement item that contains the quantification information.

Create and Define UoM Classes

To create a UoM Class, ensure that you have the Creator privilege and have the Standard locked for editing:

1.  Right-click and select Add Measure Class. You can expand a UoM Class and create a Class within it to create a

group.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 65

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

2.  Enter the required information:

3.  Enter a unique identification for the UoM Class. This supports alphanumeric characters. This field does not

auto-increment.

Note: Whilst the application accepts white spaces and special characters, it is not recommended to use
them.

a.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If left blank, ID is displayed instead of Name in

the user interface. This field accepts alphanumeric characters.

b.  Describe what this UoM Class is and what it does.

4.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This

brings you to the Details tab of that UoM Class.

5.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

6.  Enter a Group. This will create groups of similar UoM Classes. The system creates groups based on the values

provided here. The groups are displayed within the Measure Classes tree structure.

7.  Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in which the UoM Classes will be listed

in the group folder in the tree view.

8.  Enter an Aspect if it is implemented on the Unit of Measure Class. For information on Aspect, see

Understanding Aspects.

The UoM Class is now defined.

Note: To discontinue using a UoM Class, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties section on
the Details tab.

Create and Define UoM Units

To create a UoM Unit, ensure that you have the Creator privilege and have the Standard locked for editing:

1.  Right-click a parent folder and select Add Measure Unit.

2.  Enter the required information:

a.  Enter a unique identification for the UoM Unit. This supports alphanumeric characters. This field does

not auto-increment.

Note: Whilst the application accepts white spaces and special characters, it is not recommended to use
them.

b.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If left blank, it shows the ID as the name. This

field accepts alphanumeric characters.

c.  Describe the purpose of the Unit.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This

brings you to the Details tab of that UoM Unit.

4.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

5.

In the Properties Specific to Measure Unit section, enter the Symbol for the UoM Unit.

6.  Enter a value for the UoM Unit in the Standard field. This is a standard in a way that maps to the

international standards for measurement and quantification.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 66

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

7.  From the System dropdown, select the measuring system.

8.  Select a Group. This groups similar Unit of Measure Units, such as length. The groups are displayed within

the Measure Units tree structure.

9.  Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in which all Unit of Measure Classes will

be listed in the group folder in the tree view.

10.  Enter an Aspect if it is implemented on the UoM Unit. For information on Aspect, see Understanding

Aspects.

The UoM Unit is now defined.

Note: To discontinue using a UoM Unit, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties section on
the Details tab.

Associate Units of Measure

After creating and defining the UoM Classes and UoM Units, you need to associate a UoM Unit with a UoM Class
and a UoM Class with Classes and Attributes.

To associate UoM Units and UoM Classes:

1.

In a UoM Class, go to the Permissible Units tab.

2.  Click Add.

3.

In the Add Permissible Measure Units window, select the required UoM Unit from the dropdown.

4.  Click Add to create the class and continue to create classes. Alternatively, click Add and Close to add the

Class and close the window.

Handle Enumerations

Enumerations are applicable to Attributes and properties of Classes. The properties of Classes and Attributes are
dealt using Aspects. AVEVA Information Standards Manager provides you the capability to create enumerated
lists that can be applied to Attributes when required.

Create Enumerated Lists

To create an Enumerated List, ensure that you have the Creator privilege and have the Standard locked for
editing:

1.  Right-click a folder and select Add List.

2.  Enter the required information:

3.  Enter a unique identification for the Enumeration List. This supports alphanumeric characters. This field does

not auto-increment.

Note: Whilst the application accepts white spaces and special characters, it is not recommended to use
them.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 68

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

a.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If left blank, ID is displayed instead of Name in

the user interface. This field accepts alphanumeric characters.

b.  Describe the purpose of the enumerated list.

4.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This

brings you to the Details tab of that Enumeration List.

5.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

6.  Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in which all Lists will be listed in a folder

in the tree structure.

7.  Enter an Aspect if you have an enumerated list associated with Attributes. This enables you to select from a
fixed set of possible values or associations for an Attribute. For information, see Understanding Aspects.

The Enumerated List is now created.

Note: To discontinue using an Enumerated List, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties
section on the Details tab.

Create Enumerated List Items

To create an enumerated list item:

1.  Right-click an enumerated list in the folder structure and select Add List Item.

2.  Enter the required information:

a.  Enter a unique identification for the Enumeration List Item. This supports alphanumeric characters. This

field does not auto-increment.

Note: Whilst the application accepts white spaces and special characters, it is not recommended to use
them.

b.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If left blank, ID is displayed instead of Name in

the user interface. This field accepts alphanumeric characters.

c.  Describe the purpose of the enumerated list item.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This

brings you to the Details tab of that Enumeration List Item.

4.

5.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

In the Properties Specific to Lookup List Item section, the List field contains the name of the Enumerated
List name under which you are creating the list item. You may change that if required.

6.  Go to the General Properties section. Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in

which all Lists will be listed in a folder in the tree view.

The Enumerated List Item is now created and is now visible under in the Items tab of the Enumeration List.
Repeat the steps to create more Enumerated List Items.

Note: To discontinue using an Enumerated List Item, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties
section on the Details tab.
