Understand Naming and Numbering

Naming and Numbering helps you with the organization and analysis of data. You can have multiple templates on
Classes, and they are derived by their child Classes. The relationship of Naming Element and Naming Template is
such that when a Naming Element is associated with a Naming Template, the behaviour of the Naming Element
can be adjusted without disturbing the actual Element.

Naming Template:

It is a structured collection of elements that can be applied to a Class.

Naming Element:

A component of a naming template. This component must relate to an attribute.

Add Naming Elements

To create a Naming Element, ensure that you have the Creator privilege and have the Standard locked for
editing:

1.  Right-click the Naming Element folder and select Add Naming Element.

2.  Enter the required information:

a.  Enter a unique identification for the Naming Element. This supports alphanumeric characters, and does

not auto-increment.

Note: Whilst the application accepts white spaces and special characters, it is not recommended to use
them.

b.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If left blank, ID is displayed instead of Name in

the user interface. This field accepts alphanumeric characters.

c.  Describe the purpose of this Naming Element.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This
brings you to the Details tab of that Naming Element.In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details
tab, enter a nomenclature.

4.

5.

6.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

In the Properties Specific to Naming Element section, enter a number in the Sequence ID field. Any new
record will auto-increment this number.

In the Attribute field, select the source Attribute for the Element. For example, for Discipline Identifier as the
Naming Element, Discipline will be the source Attribute. You can have only one Attribute.

7.  Select the Mandatory check box if the Attribute is mandatory.

8.  Optionally, select the Hide on Empty Value check box. It is recommended to leave the check box cleared if

the Mandatory check box is selected.

9.

In the Prefix, Suffix and Padding fields, enter values for the respective fields. These values define the Naming
Element:

•  Prefix: Specifies a character sequence to prefix the Element value when composing an object name.

•  Suffix: Specifies a character sequence to suffix the Element value when composing an object name.

Typically, either prefix or suffix is used for an Element, but not both.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 71

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

•  Padding: If the value provided contains less than the required number of characters, the value will be
padded by the padding characters. The padding will be either on left or right, depending on the Data
Type of the associated Attribute.

10.  The Min Number of Characters and Max Number of Characters fields display the lower and upper

acceptable limits, respectively, of the number of characters that the field requires. If you set a value in Min
Number of Characters, ensure that you set a value in Padding. The values must be positive integers. The
maximum value must be greater than or equal to the value for the minimum characters.

11.  Enter a value in the Fixed Value field. This value represents a hard-coded value for the Element.

12.  Enter a Validation Regular Expression in its text field.

13.  Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in which all Elements will be listed in the

folder in the tree view.

14.  Enter an Aspect if it is implemented on the Element. Aspect lists similar properties in a dropdown.

The Naming Element is defined.

Note: To discontinue using a Naming Element, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties
section on the Details tab.

Add Naming Templates

To create a Naming Template, ensure that you have the Creator privilege and have the Standard locked for
editing:

1.  Right-click the Naming Template folder and select Add Naming Template.

2.  Enter the required information:

a.  Enter a unique identification for the Naming Template. This field supports alphanumeric characters, and

does not auto-increment.

b.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If you do not input any data, ID is displayed

instead of Name in the user interface.

c.  Describe what the Naming Template contains.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This

brings you to the Details tab of that Naming Template.

4.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

5.  Optionally, in the Properties Specific to Naming Template section, enter a Validation Regular Expression in

its text field.

6.  From the Applicable For dropdown, select what the regular expression will be used for. The options are:

•  Validation: Whether the template will be used for validation of a Class.

•  Allocation: Whether the template will be used for allocation.

•  Validation and Allocation: Whether the template will be used for both validation and allocation.

•  None

7.  Optionally, enter a value in the Sort Order field. The application uses this value for sorting and listing of the

Naming Templates.

8.  Enter an Aspect if you have an enumerated list associated with the Naming Template. This enables you to

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 72

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

select from a fixed set of possible values when creating records.

The Naming Template is now defined.

Note: To discontinue using a Naming Template, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties
section on the Details tab.

Associate Naming Templates and Naming Elements

To associate Naming Templates and Naming Elements:

1.  Go to the Permissible Elements tab of a Naming Template you wish to associate.

2.  Click Add.

3.

In the Add Element window, select the required Naming Element from the dropdown.

4.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close.

Applying Extensions

In the Extensions tab, you can add and manage extensions to enable mapping with third-party tools. Expand
each of the values, if available, to look at the underlying extensions. For information, see Managing Extensions.

Adding Language Translations

In the Languages tab, you can add and manage translations into different languages. The read-only list in this tab
displays the available language translations. For information, see Adding a Language Translation section.

Implement Corresponding Identifiers

When exporting or importing data from or into a data source, information discrepancies can occur even in a
simple case of misplaced plurals. Such situations can be countered by implementing naming conventions. The
naming conventions help us map the existing data with that of standardised information by making it upgrade-
proof and version-proof.

Having the same name (and case sensitivity) when upgrading to the latest version of AVEVA Information
Standards Manager ensures version-proofing of the data. The application picks and applies the settings to the
new and modified data. This is different from the concept of Languages. For information, see Adding Language
Translations.

Note: You must define a nomenclature type. You can have multiple maps for a nomenclature type.

To implement Corresponding Identifiers:

1.  Click the Details button (
the top of your screen.

) on Catalogue or select the currently Active Standard from the global toolbar at

2.  Go to the Naming Conventions tab.

3.  Click Add.

4.

In the Add Naming Convention window, enter the required information:

a.  Enter a unique identification for the naming convention. For example, ISO15926. This supports

alphanumeric characters. This field does not auto-increment.

Note: Whilst the application accepts white spaces and special characters, it is not recommended to use
them.

b.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If left blank, ID is displayed instead of Name in

the user interface. This field accepts alphanumeric characters.

c.  Provide the details of the naming convention.

5.  To create the record, click Add. This brings you back to the Naming Conventions tab.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 74

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

In addition to having identifiers, you can specify descriptive names. The application uses the value in the
Separator field to separate the identifier and the description. If this field is left blank, the colon (:) character
becomes the default separator.

6.  To reorder the records displayed in the the table, click the Sort Order value on the Naming Conventions tab

and enter a new value. The records take precedence based on their order in the table.

7.  After reviewing the details provided, click Close.

The record that you create here populates into the Corresponding Identifiers section when creating other
records, such as Classes, Attributes, Units of Measure and Enumerations.

Use Search

Searching through the application is easy. Select the required filter criteria and run a search.

The search capability provides:

•  Filter Criteria

•  Concept filter: Select this to select an entity type. The available options are Class Library root, Functional
Class, Physical Class, Document Class, General Class, Attribute Class, Taxonomies, Units of Measure,
Naming and Numbering, Life Cycle, Lookup Lists, Extension Schemas and Configurations. If the check box
is cleared, all the entity types are selected.

•  Modification filter: Select this to select an entity type. The available options are Added, Deleted,

Modified, Modified Ancestor and Modified Descendant. If the check box is cleared, all the entity types
are selected.

•  Validation filter: Select this to select an entity type. The available options are Errors, Warnings,

Suggestions, Hints, Descendant Errors, Descendant Warnings, Descendant Suggestions and Descendant
Hints. If the check box is cleared, all the entity types are selected.

•  Extensions filter: Select this to select an entity type. The available options are Naming Conventions,

Policy Rules and other third-party or plug-in validators. If the check box is cleared, all the entity types are
selected.

•  Other filters: Select this to select an entity type. The only available option is Translations. If the check

box is cleared, all the entity types are selected.

•  Filter Conditions

•  ID

•  Name

•  Extensions

•  Other

•  Search Options

•  Case Sensitive

•  Regular Expressions (Regex)

•  Invert Selection

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager

To perform search:

1.  Enter a keyword in the search text field.

2.  Select check boxes that correspond to the filter criteria. You may select more than one check box.

Note: Some of these may not be available until other functionality has been run. For example, Validation
Filters.

3.  Select from the filter conditions and search options.

Validate Standards

Validation sets the rules for data coherence. AVEVA Information Standards Manager enables you to validate a
Standard against the available validators. You can execute a validation from the Catalogue view. Validation action
is only available when the Standard is locked for editing.

After validating a Standard, the items in Standard are annotated with wavy lines of different colours representing
the severity of the issue. Items having multiple issues are annotated with a wavy line in the red colour. A
validation summary with the three most severe issues is also displayed in the tooltip for the item. You can find
the complete list of issues under Show Validation Issues in the context menu for the item.

Validation annotations are only visual and will NOT be stored as a part of the Standard. However, they may be
exported into Microsoft Excel using AVEVA Standard ISM Format and selecting the Include Validation Issues
check box.

The following items can be annotated:

•  Standard details

•  Standard collections

•  Entity tree

•  Entity tab

•  Entity details

•  Entity properties

•  Entity collections

The AVEVA ISM Naming and Numbering Rules and AVEVA ISM Policy Rules are available out of the box. The
other validators need to have the appropriate plug-in installed. To check for available validators, either select

Validate or click the information and help (

) icon and switch to the About tab.

Validation Annotations

The following is the list of validation annotations, sorted by the severest first:

of severity Error.

one or more issues of severity Error.

of severity Warning.

one or more issues of severity Warning.

of severity Suggestion.

one or more issues of severity Suggestion.

of severity Hint.

Entity has descendants, for example, a collection, with
one or more issues of severity Hint.

Manage Validation Rules

To manage validation rules:

1.  Click the Details button (

) on Catalogue and switch to the Class Library Policy Configuration tab. You can

use this tab to view and set the validation rules. This is the default setting and helps preserve data integrity
of the Standard.

2.  Click Validation Rules to display a categorized list of rules for validation:

3.  Optionally, select the Compact View check box to display validation rules in a summarized format.

4.  Expand a rule to view its underlying components. Select the required severity of validation from the

dropdown.

Note: Click the Restore to factory settings to restore the validation to the default setting.

4.  Click the Validate button.

The application begins the validation process based on the validators you may have selected.

Manage Life Cycle

This Asset Life Cycle Management capability helps you configure object-level changes in data for all its maturity
levels. You can use AVEVA Information Standards Manager to create Maturity Levels and Life Cycle Types and

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 84

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

their data relationships with Classes and Attributes.

Life Cycle:

Throughout a project's execution, an asset passes through various life cycle stages, starting from design
conceptualization to decommissioning. In information management context, you may use Life Cycle Types to
map that information from its generation to its consumption. A Life Cycle Type can be associated with more than
one Class and can contain more than one Maturity Level. But, a Class can have only one Life Cycle Type.

Maturity Level:

In the context of Life Cycle Management, the Maturity Levels establish definitive stages for progression of
information. You can use a Maturity Level to create a list of such levels at which the information may change its
form or requirements. A Maturity Level is associated with Attribute. An Attribute can have more than one
Maturity Level, while a Maturity Level can apply to more than one Attribute and to more than one Life Cycle
Type.

Add Life Cycle Types

To create a Life Cycle Type:

1.  Right-click the Life Cycle Types folder and select Add Life Cycle Type.

2.  Enter the required information:

a.  Enter a unique identification for the Maturity Level. This field supports alphanumeric characters, and

does not auto-increment.

b.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If you do not input any data, ID is displayed

instead of Name in the user interface.

c.  Describe what the Life Cycle Type contains. For example, the duration or stages.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This

brings you to the Details tab of that Life Cycle Type.

4.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

5.

In the Properties Specific to Life Cycle Type section, select a Concept from the dropdown.

6.  Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in which the Life Cycle Types are listed

in a folder in the tree view.

A Life Cycle Type is created.

Note: To discontinue using a Life Cycle Type, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties section
on the Details tab.

Define Maturity Levels

To create a Maturity Level:

1.  Right-click the Maturity Level folder and select Add Maturity Level.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 85

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

2.  Enter the required information:

a.  Enter a unique identification for the Maturity Level. This field supports alphanumeric characters, and

does not auto-increment.

b.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If you do not input any data, ID is displayed

instead of Name in the user interface.

c.  Describe what the Maturity Level contains.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This

brings you to the Details tab of that Maturity Level.

4.

5.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

In the Properties Specific to Maturity Level section, select a Discipline and Presence. The application displays
data for a combination of these fields. For the Presence field, the available options are:

•  Required: Select this for a mandatory value.

•  Preferred: Select this if it is good to have a value.

•  Optional: Select this if the absence of a value does not make a difference.

•  Not Applicable

6.  Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in which the Maturity Levels are listed in

a folder in the tree view.

A Maturity Level is created.

Note: To discontinue using a Maturity Level, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties section
on the Details tab.

Add Maturity Levels to Life Cycle

To add a Maturity Level to a Life Cycle:

1.  Go to the Permissible Maturity Levels tab. Click Add.

2.

In the Add Permissible Maturity Level window, select the require Maturity Level from the dropdown.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close.

Define the Behaviour of Attributes based on Maturity Levels

To define the behaviour of Attributes based on Maturity Levels:

1.  Select the required Attribute.

2.

In the Details tab, go to the Maturity Levels field and select a Maturity Level from the dropdown.

3.  Click the Maturity Level token to open it.

4.

In the Details tab of the selected Maturity Level, enter value for the Discipline and Presence fields.

The behaviour of the Attribute on the Maturity Level will vary based on the combination of the values
selected in the Discipline and Presence fields.

5.  Go back to the required Attribute.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 86

6.  Repeat steps 2 through 5 to add other Maturity Levels and their respective Discipline and Presence fields.

Use Custom Aspects

You can use AVEVA Information Standards Manager to create custom Aspects. The application provides a list of
out-of-the-box enumerations. However, you can use the custom Aspects to extend the capabilities of
Configuration Schemas. For information on defining and using a Configuration Schema, see Implementing
Configuration Schemas.

Step 1: Creating Custom Aspects

You can create Custom Aspects by using Enumerations and inserting the "Enumerations.<customAspect>" in the
Aspect field.

To create custom Aspects, ensure that you have the Creator privilege and have the Standard locked for editing.
The following example shows you how to create a custom Aspect:

1.

In Enumerations, right-click and select Add List.

2.  Create a look-up list by adding an identifier, name and description. For example, "Nutrients". The identifier

field is mandatory and does not auto-increment.

Note: Do not add any spaces while entering the identifier.

3.  Open the list. In the Aspect field, on the Details tab, enter "Enumerations.<Your custom Aspect>" (without

the quotes). For example, "Enumerations.Nutrients".

4.  Then, click the Add () button. This adds the enumeration to the Nutrients list. See the Aspect field in the

following:

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 94

Implement Configuration Schema

Most information management modules within AVEVA Information Standards Manager are intrinsic. While all
data is connected internally and has common properties, such as identifier, name and description, some
properties are exclusive to concepts, such as attribute definition, group UoM class and data type. Any
requirement for assigning custom properties can be done using Extensions. You can use the information in this
chapter to manage extensions.

Understand the Configuration Schema Model

A configuration schema is a structure of elements that can be visualized hierarchically. A typical configuration
schema includes the following elements: schema, extension point, section, group, property and sub group. It is
recommended to have only one schema per Standard fragment.

•  Schema: A schema element is the root in a configuration schema definition. It contains a unique identifier

that makes a schema reusable across multiple Standards.

•  Extension Point: A schema can have more than one extension point, each of which may be a container for
extensions that apply to one or more concepts. For example, if a schema has extensions that apply to both
Functional and Document Classes, you can define those extensions in the same extension point.

•  Section: The extension point can contain more than one section, each of which may be a container for
extensions that appear at a specific place or situation in the user interface. The user interface for all
extensions in a section will appear together.

•  Group: A section can contain more than one group, each of which may be a container for one or more

properties or sub groups.

•  Properties: A group may or may not contain properties. Each property is a representation of the uniqueness

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 98

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

of the user interface.

•  Sub Group: A group may contain sub groups. Each sub group can contain one or more properties. A sub

group is like a group, but it cannot contain any further sub groups.

Define Configuration Schema

To define a configuration schema, ensure that you have the Creator privilege and have the Standard locked for
editing:

1.

In Content Discovery sidebar, select Manage Schema. Then, right-click Configuration Schema and click Add
Configuration Schema.

2.  Enter the identifier, name and description, and click Create.

3.  The details of the new configuration schema are available on the Details tab.

created. You may delete it, if required, by clicking the cross (X) sign. Or, you can change its name and use it.
However, you will not be able to change its ID.

4.  Click Extension Point+ to create an extension point.

5.  Enter the identifier, name and description, and click Create.

The newly created tab (of the extension point) contains only the capability to add a section.

Activate Schema in a Standard

The schema only remains available in the composition in the context of the Standard. You need to activate the
schema for the Standard by creating at least one configuration instance.

To create an instance of the schema and to activate it for the Standard:

1.  Select Manage Configurations from the Content Discover Sidebar.

2.  Navigate to your configuration schema.

3.  Right-click the configuration schema and select Add a Configuration based on this Schema.

4.

In the Add a Configuration window, enter the identifier, name and description, and click Create.

Your configuration settings appear on the target details.

Add a Language Translation

Adding a language translation is not restricted to providing the alternative for the object name and description in
other languages. You can use this feature to map objects in different languages. This is not based on creating and
managing nomenclature maps, which are a collection of corresponding identifiers in naming conventions like ISO
15926.

To add a language:

1.

In the Culture Code dropdown, select the required code. This field is mandatory.

2.  Optionally, enter a name and its description.

3.  Click Add to create the record and continue to create more records. Alternatively, click Add and Close to add

the record and close the window.

Maintain Taxonomies

A Taxonomy is a way of structuring Concept-based information without disturbing the hierarchies laid within
Concepts.

You can use taxonomies to catalogue the information contained within objects by categorizing them in groups
and subgroups. Taxonomy provides a way of breaking down Classes into arbitrary structures that contain a
collection of Nodes. A node can further be a collection of nodes.

For detailed understanding of taxonomies, see AVEVA Information Standards Manager Importing from MS Excel
document.

What is a Taxonomy Node?

A taxonomy node is a single point, or node, within the structured order of information. It can contain a collection
of nodes. The taxonomical hierarchy can be deep. Therefore, it is recommended that the taxonomy identifiers be
unique within a taxonomy node and none of the node contain a white space (space between characters and
words).

Add a Taxonomy

To create a taxonomy using the following procedure, ensure that you have the Creator privilege and have the
Standard locked for editing:

1.  Right-click a Concept and select Add Taxonomy.

2.  Enter the required information:

a.  Enter a unique identification for the Taxonomy. This supports alphanumeric characters, and does not

auto-increment.

Note: Whilst the application accepts white spaces and special characters, it is not recommended to use
them.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 109

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

b.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If left blank, ID is displayed instead of Name in

the user interface. This field accepts alphanumeric characters.

c.  Describe the purpose of this Taxonomy.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This

brings you to the Details tab of that Taxonomy.

4.

5.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

In the Properties Specific to Taxonomy section, select a value for Concept from the dropdown. This value is
applicable only for root-level Taxonomies, such as Functional or Physical.

6.  Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in which the Taxonomies will be listed in

the tree view.

The Taxonomy is now defined.

Note: To discontinue using a Taxonomy Node, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties
section.

Add Taxonomy Nodes

To create a taxonomy using the following procedure, ensure that you have the Creator privilege and have the
Standard locked for editing:

1.  Right-click a Taxonomy and select Taxonomy Node.

2.  Enter the required information:

a.  Enter a unique identification for the Taxonomy Node. This supports alphanumeric characters, and does

not auto-increment.

Note: Whilst the application accepts white spaces and special characters, it is not recommended to use
them.

b.  Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If left blank, ID is displayed instead of Name in

the user interface. This field accepts alphanumeric characters.

c.  Describe the purpose of this Taxonomy Node.

3.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close. This

brings you to the Details tab of that Taxonomy Node.

4.

5.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a nomenclature map. You can enter more
than one nomenclature map. For information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

In the Properties Specific to Taxonomy Node section, select a category or discipline taxonomy, if available, in
the Taxonomy field and a parent taxonomy in the Ancestor field. However, if the extensions are suffixed with
an exclamation mark (!), then the Ancestor field appears empty. For a Class Concept, this section appears as
Properties Specific to Permissible <Class Concept> Class.

6.  Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in which the Taxonomy Nodes are listed

in the tree view.

The Taxonomy Node is now created.

Notes:

•  To discontinue using a Taxonomy Node, select the Obsolete check box in the General Properties section on

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 110

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

the Details tab.

•  Ensure that the taxonomy name does not contain the pipe character (|). Out of multiple segments delimited
by the pipe character, the first segment is the taxonomy identifier, while the successive segments are node
identifiers.

Assigning default value on a node ensures that within some consuming application, you can use a value that you
have defined using extensions.

To assign an Attribute's default value:

2.  Click the Add (

) button.

a.  Attribute: The list of Attributes is derived based on the Discipline.

b.  Measure Unit: This field is mandatory if the Value field is left blank.

c.  Value: This field is mandatory if the Measure Unit field is left blank.

4.  To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add. Alternatively, click Add and Close.

Import from MS Excel

This section describes the requirements set by AVEVA Information Standards Manager™ (AVEVA ISM) while
reading Class Library definitions from a Microsoft® Excel® (Excel or MS Excel) workbook, according to ISM
Standard Excel Format v1.2.0.

This section is accompanied by ISM Standard Excel Format v1.2.0.xlsx, which is an Excel workbook and contains
examples of each of the sheets explained here.

When loading Class Library content from an Excel workbook, AVEVA ISM can load the content into an existing
Class Library model. Alternatively, you can create a new model based on the workbook content. You may also
load multiple workbooks, one at a time, into the same resulting Class Library model.

Rules for Excel Workbook

•  The class library content has to be provided in a Microsoft Excel 2007-2013 (*.xslx) workbook format. The

workbook may be provided as a single file in a zip archive.

•  Sheet names and column headers (row 1) are not case sensitive.

•  The reader will look for sheet names that it recognizes, and recognized column headers in those sheets, to

extract the class library content.

•  All sheets are optional, but if some are missing the resulting Class Library model might not constitute a

consistent Class Library model.

•  Sheet names, or column names, that are not recognized will be ignored.

•  The sheets might be distributed over multiple workbooks, which can be loaded in sequence to extend the

same Class Library model instance.

•  Required column headers that cannot be found will be reported on.

•  Rows not containing a value in a required column will be reported on.

•  Some columns may be constrained to only contain values in certain ranges (for example, Y/N, or true/false).

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 113

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

Violation of such constraints will be reported on.

•  Rows containing empty cells only will be ignored.

•  Empty cells will be ignored, unless it represents a required value.

•  When loading a workbook into an existing Class Library model, cells containing the text {null} will clear the

corresponding property in the resulting Class Library model.

•  The order of the sheets in the workbook, and the order of the columns in a sheet, are not significant.

•  All rows in all sheets might include a column _ACTION, where certain values will have specific meaning:

•  Ignore: The corresponding spread sheet row will be ignored.

•  Note: The corresponding spread sheet row is considered to contain a note, and will therefore be ignored.

•  Delete: When loading into an existing Class Library model, the definition corresponding to current row

will be deleted. If no such definition exists, the row will be ignored.

•  Other values in this column will be reported on.

Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA ISM Intrinsic Definitions

Explanation to the column headers in the sections to follow:

•  Column Name: Describes the name of a column header in the corresponding spread sheet. The names are

case insensitive, but they have to be spelled exactly as stated (including spaces and punctuations).

•  Constraint:

•  Column Header: Means that the column header has to be in the spread sheet, but the rows does not

necessarily have to contain a value for the column.

•  Required: Means that the column header has to be in the spread sheet, and all rows will have to contain

a valid value for the column.

•  Italic Text: Represents a valid value for the column

•  Multi-lingual definitions: Most of the definitions in the Class Library contain the properties Name and

Description. The values provided for these properties should be in the default language (of your choice) of
the Class Library. In addition you can add arbitrary columns in the spread sheets for providing Name and
Description in additional languages. These column headers should be named "Name {language}" and
"Description {language}", where {language} is composed as "{languageCode}-{countryCode}". For example,
Name in Norwegian should have the column header "Name nb-NO"

•  The first argument is a valid ISO Language Code. These codes are the lower- case two-letter codes as

defined by ISO-639-1

•  The second argument is a valid ISO Country Code. These codes are the upper-case two-letter codes as

defined by ISO-3166

Sheet: 'ISM Extension Namespaces'

Every definition in a Class Library, such as Attributes, Functional Classes, Enumerations, support a predefined set
of intrinsic properties, such as Id, Name and Description. You may extend any of these definitions with arbitrary
properties of our choice. To do so, you will first have to define an extension namespace and then associate your
arbitrary properties with this namespace. This sheet defines extension namespaces.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

You can add extension properties to any of the spread sheets described in this chapter, by adding columns
headers according to the syntax:

•  {prefix}:[Node1/…/NodeN/]{propertyName}

Assume you have defined a namespace (with prefix mapping) for mapping attribute names to nomenclatures
used by different software systems or industry standards. Then you would typically extend the sheet Sheet: 'ISM
Attributes' with column headers like:

•  mapping:CFIHOS/name

•  mapping:Maximo/name

•  mapping:ISO15926/name

Rules and impact of extensions are dictated by the intention of the target extension namespace. In most cases
this comes down to how the extensions are being adopted by the consuming software. To be on the safe side,
you should assume that extension node and property names are case sensitive.

If a definition (for example, a Functional Class) is extended by another definition, any extensions defined on the
ancestor will by default be derived by the descendants. By suffixing an extension property name by exclamation
mark (for example, mapping:ISO15926/name!), you qualify the property to not be derivable, and it will not be
derived by descendant definitions.

combined they will.
Hence they should be
annotated with
Abstract=true

•  Typically a deployable

Class Library is
targeting a specific
Asset (or Plant),
covering
requirements specific
to the target Asset.
The Class Library
fragment
representing these
Asset specific
requirements will
include all the other
fragments as a
composition, and
should be considered
a concrete definition
(Abstract=false)

•  This setting will
dictate certain
behaviour of AVEVA
ISM:

•  A fragment can
only extend
another fragment

•  A fragment
cannot be
checked for
consistency by
the AVEVA ISM
consistency
validators

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Column Name

Constraint

Description

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

•  A fragment

cannot be used
for generating
software
configuration
artefacts in
AVEVA ISM

Annotating an Attribute
with an aspect is a way to
associate the Attribute
with a specific semantic
definition. Application

associated processing

When evolving a Class
Library over time,
removing previously
existing definitions might
cause issues with
consuming software
systems (for example,
they might not support
deleting an Attribute that
is in use for existing data).
Obsoleting deprecated
definitions might
therefore be a less
intrusive approach. How,
or whether, obsoleting is
implemented might vary
across consuming
software though

SortOrder

systems might also
utilise this
information to decide
on in which order
Attribute values are to
be presented

Sheet: 'ISM Functional Classes'

This sheet defines Functional Classes and Sub Classes (or Tag Classes).

An abstract class, "Root Functional Class" (@id="ISM_ROOT_FUNCTIONAL"), will be created automatically as the
root of the class hierarchy, with all top level classes (classes with no "Extends") extending this class. You are free
to define properties for this root class, by including a class definition with Id=" ISM_ROOT_ FUNCTIONAL" in this
sheet.

identifier values do

Sheet: 'ISM Functional Class Attributes'

This sheet defines association of Permissible Attributes (or Characteristics) with Functional Classes.

If you have Permissible Attributes common to all Functional Classes, you can specify them as permissible on a
class with Id="ISM_ROOT_ FUNCTIONAL" (see Sheet: 'ISM Functional Classes'), and they will automatically be
derived as permissible on all Functional Classes.

Sheet: 'ISM Physical Classes'

This sheet defines Physical Classes and Sub Classes (or Equipment Classes).

An abstract class, "Root Physical Class" (@id="ISM_ROOT_PHYSICAL"), will be created automatically as the root
of the class hierarchy, with all top level classes (classes with no "Extends") extending this class. You are free to
define properties for this root class, by including a class definition with Id=" ISM_ROOT_ PHYSICAL" in this sheet.

not contain white

•  To avoid identifier
naming conflicts in
applications
implementing the

within a Class Library.

Sheet: 'ISM Document Classes'

This sheet defines Document Classes. An abstract class, "Root Document

Class" (@id="ISM_ROOT_DOCUMENT"), will be created automatically as the root of the class hierarchy, with all
top level classes (classes with no "Extends") extending this class. You are free to define properties for this root
class, by including a class definition with Id=" ISM_ROOT_DOCUMENT" in this sheet.

Item

1

Column Name

Constraint

Description

Id

Required

•  The identifier for the

Class definition. There
is a requirement that
this value is unique
within the collection
of Document Classes
in the target Class
Library model

•  Even though it is not

an absolute
requirement, it is
recommended that
identifier values do
not contain white
space characters

concepts, are unique
within a Class Library.
This can easily be
achieved by using a
naming convention,
such as "DOC_nnnn".

•  It is strongly

recommended, when
a Class Library evolves
over time, to never
change the value of
any identifiers.

Sheet: 'ISM General Classes'

This sheet defines General Classes and Sub Classes. Classes that do not fall into one of the categories Functional,
Physical or Document. For example, System, Area and Contractor.

An abstract class, "Root General Class" (@id="ISM_ROOT_GENERAL"), will be created automatically as the root
of the class hierarchy, with all top level classes (classes with no "Extends") extending this class. You are free to
define properties for this root class, by including a class definition with Id=" ISM_ROOT_ GENERAL" in this sheet.

Item

1

Column Name

Constraint

Description

Id

Required

•  The identifier for the

Class definition. There
is a requirement that
this value is unique
within the collection
of General Classes in
the target Class
Library model.

•  Even though it is not

an absolute
requirement, it is
recommended that
identifier values do
not contain white
space characters.

•  To avoid identifier
naming conflicts in
applications
implementing the
Class Library it is
recommended that all
identifiers, across all
concepts, are unique
within a Class Library.
This can easily be
achieved by using a
naming convention,
such as "TAG_nnnn".

•  It is strongly

recommended, when
a Class Library evolves
over time, to never
change the value of
any identifiers.

containing pattern
matching regular
expressions will only
be applicable for
Validation

•  It is possible to

specify more than
one value, in which
case the values need
to be separated by a
single space character

Annotating a Unit with an
aspect is a way to
associate the Unit with a
specific semantic
definition. Application
logic will look for these
aspects, and apply
associated processing
rules. Definition of
available aspect values is
outside the scope of this
document.

When evolving a Class
Library over time,
removing previously
existing definitions might
cause issues with
consuming software
systems (for example,
they might not support
deleting a Unit that is in
use for existing data).
Obsoleting deprecated
definitions might
therefore be a less
intrusive approach. How,
or whether, obsoleting is
supported might vary
across consuming
software though.

in the AVEVA ISM user
interface.

Units not having a value
specified for this property
will be ordered as if they
have the highest
SortOrder value.

Sheet: 'ISM Enumerations'

This sheet defines Enumeration Lists with Items (also called List-Of-Values), typically used for constraining valid
values for Attributes.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 178

Item

1

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

Column Name

Constraint

Description

Id

Required

•  An Enumeration is a
List with Items, and
potentially sub-Items.
The rows in this sheet
therefore either
represent a List, or
they represent an
Item belonging to a
list.

•  If the value provided
do not contain a "|"
(pipe), the entire
value is considered a
List identifier. There is
a requirement that
List identifiers are
unique within the
collection of
Enumeration Lists in
the target Class
Library model

•  Even though it is not

an absolute
requirement, it is
recommended that
List identifier values
do not contain white
space characters

•  It is strongly

recommended, when
a Class Library evolves
over time, to never
change the value of
any identifiers

•  If the value provided
consists of multiple
segments delimited
by "|" (pipe), the first
segment is considered
the List identifier,
while the successive
segments is
considered Item
identifiers, optionally

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 179

Item

Column Name

Constraint

Description

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

sub-Item identifiers.

•  An Enumeration List
can typically be used
as a ValidationRule for
an Attribute; to
constrain what values
will be accepted for
the corresponding
attribute. An
information authoring
application
consuming the Class
Library might choose
to render the List as a
drop-down list in the
user interface. When
a user makes a
selection in this list, it
is typically the
identifier value for the
corresponding Item
that is considered the
data value. Therefore
the notation, and the
rules, for Item
identifiers are
different from other
identifier values in the
Class Library. So
instead of using a
notation like
"ITEM_nnn", you
should specify the
data value that the
item represents. For
example, a List that
represents possible
"Cooling Types" might
have Items with
identifiers like "AIR",
"WATER".

A human readable name
for the List or Item
definition

Sheet: 'ISM N&N Elements'

This sheet defines Naming & Numbering Elements, to be used when composing Naming Templates.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 182

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

As an example, a Tag name is typically a composition of multiple values. The rule for each of these values is
defined by an Element. A Naming Template is then defined as a collection of Elements, which in sum defines all
the aspects of the Tag name. An example from the NORSOK Coding System describes the Naming Template for
lines consisting of the following Elements:

The interpretation of the rules described for an Element might vary across different implementing software
applications. The description below is based on how the AVEVA Engineering Numbering Clock is implementing
the rules.

typically not practical
to rename these
existing objects.

applicable for

then be used to
validate the names of
existing objects, while
naming templates
applicable for

used when allocating
names to new
objects.

case the values need
to be separated by a

Sheet: 'ISM Life Cycle Types'

This sheet contains sequences of Maturity Levels representing a life cycle.

The main purpose of Life Cycle Types is to support the assumption that different data objects (of different
classes) might have different number of maturity gates (applicable Maturity Levels) in their life cycle. By
associating a Life Cycle Type with a Sheet: 'ISM Functional Classes' or a Sheet: 'ISM Document Classes', one can
predict what maturity gates objects of those classes will have to pass in their life cycle.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

1

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

Column Name

Constraint

Description

Id

Required

•  The identifier for the

Life Cycle Type
definition. There is a
requirement that this
value is unique within
the collection of Life
Cycle Types in the
target Class Library
model.

•  Even though it is not

an absolute
requirement, it is
recommended that
identifier values do
not contain white
space characters.

•  To avoid identifier
naming conflicts in
applications
implementing the
Class Library it is
recommended that all
identifiers, across all
concepts, are unique
within a Class Library.
This can easily be
achieved by using a
naming convention,
such as "LCT_nnnn".

•  It is strongly

recommended, when
a Class Library evolves
over time, to never
change the value of
any identifiers.

Annotation of Intrinsic Definition for AVEVA AIM Consumption

AVEVA AIM™ extensions are defined in the namespace:

•  Prefix: anet

•  Uri: http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/Class.Library/Model/AVEVA_NET/2012/11

In addition to the dedicated sheets targeting AVEVA AIM configurations, it is also possible to annotate some of
the intrinsic definitions with aspects for configuring AVEVA AIM.

To further control how AVEVA AIM is configured, you can also extend intrinsic definitions with an arbitrary
number of "expando" columns:

•  An expando column header has to be prefixed by the AVEVA AIM

•  extension namespace prefix.

•  The expando column header (without the prefix) will be added as a property to the target construct in the

AVEVA AIM Class Library.

•  As an example, if a column header "anet:Scale" is added to the sheet "ISM UoM Class Units", the value in

this column will result in <Scale>{column value}</ Scale> being added to the permissible unit for the target
Measure Class definition in the AVEVA AIM Class Library.

Suppress entities from being consumed by AVEVA AIM

By annotating an entity with the extension property anet:suppress="true", the entity will be suppressed from the
AVEVA AIM configuration. This property is applicable for entities in the following sheets:

•  ISM Attributes

•  ISM Functional Classes

•  ISM Functional Class Attributes

•  ISM Document Classes

•  ISM Document Class Attributes

•  ISM UoM Units

•  ISM UoM Classes

•  ISM UoM Class Units

For example, if you do not want the automatically generated classes ISM_ROOT_FUNCTIONAL (see Sheet: 'ISM
Functional Classes') and ISM_ROOT_DOCUMENT (see Sheet: 'ISM Document Classes') to be included in the
AVEVA AIM class hierarchies, you can add these class definitions to the respective sheets manually, and then
annotate them with anet:suppress="true". To prevent this annotation from being derived by child classes,
remember to suffix the column name with an exclamation mark, for example, anet:suppress! (see Sheet: 'ISM
Class Library Header'). The result will be that these classes are suppressed from the AVEVA AIM configuration,
but their child classes will still be included. The child classes will then by default get the parent class id of the
suppressed parent as their parent class id.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA AIM Extensions

These sheets contains configuration that will be utilized by the AVEVA AIM

Configurator when generating Class Library configuration targeting AVEVA AIM.

In addition to the columns explicitly described, each sheet can also contain an arbitrary number of "expando"
columns:

•  An expando column header has to be prefixed by the AVEVA AIM extension namespace prefix.

•  The expando column header (without the prefix) will be added as a property to the target construct in the

AVEVA AIM Class Library.

•  As an example, if a column header "anet:NonSearchable" is added to the sheet "ISM ANWH Classes", the

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 224

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

value in this column will result in <NonSearchable>{column value}</NonSearchable> being added to the
class definition in the AVEVA AIM Class Library.

Configure Permissible Grid Reports

This section describes how to configure and export a Permissible Grid report output from AVEVA Information
Standards Manager.

A Permissible Grid forms a matrix of Classes (vertically) and Attributes (horizontally), with the intersection
providing characteristics of their associations. The following is a sample screenshot of the output in Microsoft
Excel format:

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 232

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

The Permissible Grid report can be configured in two ways:

•  Via the user interface

•  Via XML

The most common permissible grid report configuration options are available in the user interface. For a broader
range of configuration options XML can be used.

Glossary

Abbreviations and terms used in this section:

Term

AVEVA ISM

Configuration Instance

Definition

AVEVA Information Standards Manager

An entry in the AVEVA ISM Catalogue, typically
referred to as a Class Library. When the entry contains
Permissible Grid configuration only (that is, no
Attributes, no Classes, no UoMs, an so on.), we refer
to it as a Configuration Instance.

Report Configuration via the User Interface

The most common permissible grid report configuration options are available in the user interface.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 233

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

To configure and export the Navigate to the export dialog box and select ‘AVEVA ISM Permissible Grid’ in the
‘Export As’ in the drop-down menu.

Permissible grid report configuration options

Report Format, Language & Naming Convention

Format

Select whether to export the default permissible grid as configured in the user interface or a custom one defined
in an XML schema.

Language

Select the language to export in. If no language translation is present for a class then the default value will be
exported.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 234

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

Naming Convention

Select the naming convention to export in. If no naming convention is present for a class then the class library
native value will be exported.

Content & Layout

Note: Fields with an * are mandatory.

Class Concept

Select the concept of the classes to be exported.

Life Cycle Type

Select a Life Cycle Type to filter available selections in ‘Maturity Level’.

Maturity Level

The ‘Presence’ indicator in the Class/Attribute intersection will reflect selected Maturity Level.

Class Hierarchy

Select how the class hierarchy should appear in the output.

Suppress Single Root Class

Specifies whether to suppress an abstract single root class from being exported.

Suppress Top Level Abstract

Specifies whether to suppress top-level abstract classes from being exported.

Colour Theme

Select the colour theme for the output.

Include Column Filters

Specifies whether to automatically create filter control on all column headers.

Attributes Axis

Headers to Include

Specify the attribute related headers to be included in the output.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 235

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

Order by

Specify what to order attributes on.

Permissible Only

Specifies whether to include only attributes that are permissible for the exported classes.

Break

Specify whether to add a break of an empty column between the ‘Order by’ criteria.

Classes Axis

Class Headers to Include

Specify the class related headers to be included in the output.

Order By

Specify what to order classes on.

Break

Specify whether to add a break of an empty row or a new sheet between the ‘Order by’ criteria.

Report Configuration via XML

For a broader range of configuration options, it is possible to configure the report using XML Configurations for a
report are contained in an XML Class Library definition, expressed as AVEVA ISM extensions, using a designated
extension namespace. This implies that configurations can be embedded within a Class Library definition, or they
can be kept in dedicated Class Library instances (containing nothing but report configuration information),
referred to as Configuration Instances.

The XML schema

There is an XML schema, Aveva.GEICL.Syntax.PermGrid.xsd available. If you are using a schema aware XML
editor (for example Microsoft Visual Studio, XMLSpy, or similar), installing this schema definition will improve the
editing experience significantly.

The Permissible Grid extension namespace

The extension namespace for Permissible Grid configurations is defined as:

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 236

URI

Prefix

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/
Engineering.Information/Class.Library/Reports/
PermissibleGrid/2015/01

ism-pg

The URI is case sensitive, and has to be expressed exactly as stated above. The Prefix can actually be a value of
your choice, as long as it is not conflicting with other namespace prefixes in the context (that is, Class Library)
that the configuration is targeting. The recommendation is to use the value described above though.

In XML syntax the namespace declaration will look like:

<ClassLibrary xmlns="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/
Class.Library/Model/Basic/2012/01"
xmlns:geicl="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/Class.Library/Model/
Extension/2013/12"
xmlns:ism-pg="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/Class.Library/
Reports/PermissibleGrid/2015/01">
...
<ExtensionNamespaces>
<ExtensionNamespace prefix="ism-pg"
name="AVEVA ISM Permissible Grid"
uri="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/Class.Library/Reports/
PermissibleGrid/2015/01"/>
</ExtensionNamespaces>
</ClassLibrary>

The <ExtensionNamespace> declaration is not required, but by including it the AVEVA ISM user interface will
display the "friendly" name as opposed to the less readable URI in the extensions dialogue:

The structure of a report configuration

A report configuration is split into different sections, each section describing dedicated aspects of the report
result. In compressed XML syntax the below example shows the sections of an overall configuration:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8" ?>
<ClassLibrary xmlns="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/
Class.Library/Model/Basic/2012/01" xmlns:geicl="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/
Engineering.Information/Class.Library/Model/Extension/2013/12" xmlns:ism-
pg="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/Class.Library/Reports/
PermissibleGrid/2015/01"
id="permissible.grid.01" name="01. Permissible Grid Default" version="1.0.0.0"
versionDate="2015.01.18">

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 237

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

<Extension>
<PermissibleGrids xmlns="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/
Class.Library/Reports/PermissibleGrid/2015/01">
<PermissibleGrid geicl:id="my.permissible.grid" name="My Permissible Grid" style="Default"
headerStyle="DefaultHeader">
<Variables>
<Variable geicl:id="" value=""/>
…
</Variables>
<Setup>
<Variables>
<Variable geicl:id="" value="" description="" …/>
…
</Variables>
<InputPanels>
<InputPanel geicl:id="" name="" description="" …/>
<Variables>
<Variable geicl:id="" value="" description="" …/>
…
</Variables>
</InputPanel>
…
</InputPanels>
<Conditions>
<Condition geicl:id="" apply="" />
<Variables>
<Variable geicl:id="" value="" description="" …/>
…
</Variables>
</Condition>
…
</Conditions>
</Styles>
<Styles>
<Style geicl:id="Default">
…
</Style>
</Styles>
<Page>
<Headers style="">
<Header geicl:id="" style="" value="" />
</Headers>
<Trailers style="">
<Trailer geicl:id="" style="" value="" />
</Trailer>
</Trailers>
</Page>
<Attributes permissibleOnly="true" where="">
<OrderBys style="" headerStyle="">
<OrderBy geicl:id="GroupId" condition="./@id" direction="Ascending" break="None" value=""
header="" style="" headerStyle="" />
</OrderBys>
<Headers style="" headerStyle="">
<Header geicl:id="Concept" mergeCells="true" value="" header="" style="" headerStyle="" />
</Headers>
<Columns styleWhenPresent="" styleWhenAbsent="">

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 238

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

<Column geicl:id="PresenceMarker" valueWhenPresent="" valueWhenAbsent=""
styleWhenPresent="" styleWhenAbsent="" />
</Columns>
</Attributes>
<Classes concept="Functional" classHierarchy="Expanded" where="" autoFilter="true">
<OrderBys style="" headerStyle="">
<OrderBy geicl:id="Id" condition="./@id" direction="Ascending" break="None" value=""
header="" style="" headerStyle="" />
</OrderBys>
<Columns style="" headerStyle="TagHeader">
<Column geicl:id="ClassId" value="" header="" style="" headerStyle="" />
</Columns>
</ Classes >
</PermissibleGrid>
</PermissibleGrids>
</Extension>
</ClassLibrary>

There are certain behaviors in AVEVA ISM that you should be aware of when working with Permissible Grid
configurations.

The best way of editing a Permissible Grid configuration is to use a text editor, preferably one that is XML aware.
A typical workflow in developing a configuration is to edit the XML file and load it into a catalogue item in AVEVA
ISM (preferably an autonomous catalogue item, but it can also be merged into an already existing Class Library
definition).

When you test the configuration, and find discrepancies or need to apply enhancements, you go back to the
original XML file and apply your changes and merge it back into the previously loaded catalogue item. The way
AVEVA ISM behaves when you load a file into an existing instance is that new nodes are appended at the end of
their containing collection, and properties deleted from the file are still preserved. You will experience that in
certain scenarios this might give a surprising result.

To control order of nodes, where significant, AVEVA recommends that you use the @geicl:sortOrder property.
For example, when you insert new Classes/Columns nodes, they will be appended to the existing collection,
independent of where they appear in the XML file, which means new Columns will appear at the end of the
collection and will render at the end of the columns list in the resulting report. If you apply the @geicl:sortOrder
property though, you are in total control of the order of the columns in the resulting report.

If you amend the configuration file by removing a property on a node, let’s say you remove the Classes/Columns/
Columns/@suppress property, when you reload the XML file by merging it into the existing instance, the
property does not go away! When merging an XML file into an existing instance, AVEVA ISM will apply changes to
existing definitions being loaded, add new definition being loaded, but will not delete definition not being
loaded.

If you are merging into an autonomous instance (i.e. an instance containing the Permissible Configuration only),
the recommendation is to clear out the content (an icon in the catalogue view toolbar) before merging the
amended XML file. This way you are deleting all existing content, and only the loaded content will preserve.

If you are merging into a composite instance, open the Class Library details dialogue, go the the Extensions tab,
and delete the target permissible grid configuration before merging the amended XML file.

ClassLibrary

In the current version AVEVA ISM supports two ways of organizing XML Permissible Grid configurations:

•  Embedded in the target Class Library definition

With this approach, the report configuration will be an extension to the Class Library, as any other extension.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 239

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

•  As a separate "Class Library" instance (recommended)

Even though a "Class Library" instance containing only a Permissible Grid configuration do not contain actual
Class Library content, such as classes, attributes, and so on, it will be a separately named and versioned
AVEVA ISM Catalogue item.

Note: A future version of AVEVA ISM might support multiple item types in the Catalogue, which will make it
easier to separate Configurations from Class Libraries.

For this approach, the Class Library details, such as name, version, and so on will be properties describing the
Configuration instance. AVEVA recommends that you apply these properties like you apply them for a real
Class Library, that is, a globally unique identifier, a descriptive name, a real version number, and so on.

<ClassLibrary xmlns="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/
Class.Library/Model/Basic/2012/01"
xmlns:geicl="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/Class.Library/
Model/Extension/2013/12"
xmlns:ism-pg="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/Class.Library/
Reports/PermissibleGrid/2015/01"
id=" my.permissible.grid.mycompanydomainname "
name="My Permissible Grid with Page Break"
version="1.0.0.0"
versionDate="2015.02.16">
<Extension>

A report configuration organized as a separate instance can potentially be used for multiple Class Libraries.

PermissibleGrids

The PermissibleGrids element is the root of the Permissible Grid configurations. It can take one or more
PermissibleGrid child elements.

<Extension>
<PermissibleGrids xmlns="http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/
Class.Library/Reports/PermissibleGrid/2015/01">
<PermissibleGrid geicl:id="my.permissible.grid.mycompanydomain"
name="Default Permissible Grid "
style="Default" headerStyle="DefaultHeader">

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Variables

This section is optional and is used to define a collection of named values, that is, Variables. The value of the
variables can be referenced elsewhere in the configuration, to control how the report renders.

<Variables>
<Variable geicl:id="includePermissibleAttributesOnly" value="true"/>
<Variable geicl:id="flattenTheClassHierarchy" value="true"/>
</Variables>

For explanation of the details of this section, see Variables.

Setup

This section is optional and is used to define input fields for the report export dialogue in the user interface. This
section can also contain conditional Variable definitions, based on user input in the export dialogue.

<Setup>
<Variables>
<Variable geicl:id="List_Concepts">
<ValidValues>
<ValidValue geicl:id="Functional" name="Functional Classes" geicl:sortOrder="10"/>
…
</ValidValues>
</Variable>
<Variable geicl:id="List_Class_Hierarchy">
<ValidValues>…</ValidValues>
</Variable>

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 243

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

</Variables>
<InputPanels>
<InputPanel geicl:id ="Main" name="'Content &amp; Layout'">
<Variables>
<Variable geicl:id="Input_Concept" name="'Select Concept'" description="'Select the concept
of classes to be rendered in the report'"
required="true" geicl:sortOrder="10" value="Functional" validValues="$List_Concepts[]"
consolidate="true" />
<Variable geicl:id="Input_Permissible_Only" name="'Permissible Attributes Only'"
dataType="Boolean" geicl:sortOrder="20" value="true" />
<Variable geicl:id="Input_MaturityLevel" name="'Select Maturity Level'"
geicl:sortOrder="30"
validValues="./MaturityLevels" disabled="./MaturityLevels.isNull()" />
<Variable geicl:id="Input_Class_Hierarchy" name="'Class Hierarchy'" description="'...'"
required="true" geicl:sortOrder="40" value="Flattened"
validValues="$List_Class_Hierarchy[]"/>
<Variable geicl:id="Input_Theme" name="'Select Colour Theme'"
required="true" dataType="Color" geicl:sortOrder="60" value="$Theme_Blue_Header_Color">
<ValidValues>
<ValidValue geicl:id="Blue" value="$Theme_Blue_Header_Color" name="'Blue'"/>
…
</ValidValues>
</Variable>
</InputPanel>
</InputPanels>
<Conditions apply="Exclusive">
<Condition geicl:id="Theme_Green" where="$Input_Theme == $Theme_Green_Header_Color"
geicl:sortOrder="10">
<Variables>
<Variable geicl:id="Theme_Header_Color" value="$Theme_Green_Header_Color" />
<Variable geicl:id="Theme_Header_TextColor" value="$Theme_Green_Header_TextColor" />
<Variable geicl:id="Theme_TextColor" value="$Theme_Green_TextColor" />
</Variables>
</Condition>
<Condition geicl:id="Theme_Amber" where="$Input_Theme == $Theme_Amber_Header_Color"
geicl:sortOrder="20">
<Variables>…</Variables>
</Condition>
<Condition …></Condition>
</Conditions>
</Setup>
Styles

This section is optional and is used to define a collection of Named Styles. A named style can be referenced
elsewhere in the configuration, to be applied to a given selection of cells in the grid.

<Styles>
<Style geicl:id="Default">
<Alignment horizontal="Left" vertical="Center" textRotation="0" shrinkToFit="false"
wrapText="false"/>
<Borders diagonalUp="true" diagonalDown="true">
<Bottom style="Thin" color="rgb(208,206,206)" />
<Left style="Thin" color="rgb(208,206,206)" />
<Right style="Thin" color="rgb(208,206,206)" />
<Top style="Thin" color="rgb(208,206,206)" />
</Borders>
<Fill patternColor="#fcfcfc" pattern="Solid" />

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 244

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

<Font name="Calibri" size="10" bold="false" italic="false" underline="None"
color="#ff000000" />
<NumberingFormat format="@" />
</Style>
<Style geicl:id="DefaultHeader" extends="Default">
<Fill patternColor="rgb(0,0,0)" pattern="Solid" />
<Font bold="true" color="#ffffff"/>
</Style>
<Styles>

The values specified for the different properties can be literal strings (for example Center, true, and so on.), or
they can be expressions (see Context nodes and expression syntax). Any expression in the Styles section are
resolved in the context of the Class Library root node (see Context nodes).

For explanation of the details of this section, see Named styles.

Page Header and Trailer

This section is optional, and contains two sub-sections, one describing header rows that will be rendered above
the grid in each sheet of the workbook, and the other describing trailer rows that will be rendered below the
grid in each sheet of the workbook.

<Page>
<Headers>
<Header geicl:id="Name" style="PageHeader" value="./@name ?? ./@id" />
<Header geicl:id="Blank Line" style="Default" value="' '"/>
</Headers>
<Trailers>
<Trailer geicl:id="Blank Line" style="Default" value="' '"/>
<Trailer geicl:id="Version" style="PageTrailer" value="'Version '.concat(./@version)" />
</Trailers>
</Page>

For explanation of the details of this section, see Page Header and Trailer.

Attributes

This section contains three sub-sections, one describing the ordering of the attribute headers (running
horizontally across the top of the grid), one describing values and formatting characteristics of the attribute
headers, and the third describing values and formatting characteristics of the cells in the intersection between
classes and attributes.

<Attributes permissibleOnly="$includePermissibleAttributesOnly">
<OrderBys>
<OrderBy geicl:id="GroupId" condition="./@groupId ?? ZZZZ" direction="Ascending" />
<OrderBy geicl:id="Name" condition="./@name" direction="Ascending" />
</OrderBys>
<Headers style="AttributeHeader" headerStyle="AttributeLegend">
<Header geicl:id="Group" header="'Attribute Group &#x2192;'" value="./@groupId ?? 'Misc'"
mergeCells="true" />
<Header geicl:id="Name" header="'Attribute Name &#x2192;'" value="./@name">
<Style>
<Alignment textRotation="90"/>
<Font bold="false"/>
</Style>
</Header>

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 245

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

</Headers>
<Columns styleWhenPresent="PermissiblePresent" styleWhenAbsent="PermissibleAbsent">
<Column geicl:id="PresenceMarker" valueWhenPresent="'X'" />
</Columns>
</Attributes>

For explanation of the details of this section, see Attributes.

Classes

This section contains two sub-sections, one describing the ordering of the class headers (running vertically down
the left side of the grid), and the other describing values and formatting characteristics of the class headers.

<Classes concept="Functional" flattenHierarchy="$flattenTheClassHierarchy"
where="./@name.isNotNull()">
<OrderBys>
<OrderBy geicl:id="Extends" condition="./@extendsId ?? ZZZ" direction="Ascending"
break="Row"
value="concat(./@extendsId, ' - ', ./@extends)" header="' '">
<Style>
<Font size="11" bold="true" color="rgb(0,0,0)"/>
</Style>
<HeaderStyle>
<Borders>
<Bottom style="None"/>
<Top style="Double"/>
</Borders>
<Fill pattern="None"/>
</HeaderStyle>
</OrderBy>
<OrderBy geicl:id="Name" condition="./@name" direction="Ascending"/>
</OrderBys>
<Columns headerStyle="TagHeader" style="TagColumn">
<Column geicl:id="Sub Classes" header="'Functional Classes/Sub Classes'"
value="concat(./@id, ' - ', ./@name)" />
</Columns>
</Classes>

For explanation of the details of this section, see Classes

Context nodes and expression syntax

A report configuration will contain instructions about what values to include from the target Class Library
definition. These instructions come in form of Value expressions. To understand these expressions, it is beneficial
to have a basic understanding of the Class Library node graph. Think of the Class Library as a collection of
connected nodes, where a simplified view might look something like this:

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 246

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

The diagram above can be explained as:

•  A Class Library has a collection of pre-defined properties

•  A Class Library has a collection of Functional Classes

•  The collection of Functional Classes might contain one or more Functional Class definitions

•  A Functional Class definition has a collection of pre-defined Properties

•  A Functional Class definition has a collection of permissible Attributes

•  The collection of permissible Attributes might contain one or more permissible Attribute definitions

•  A permissible Attribute has a collection of pre-defined Properties

•  A Functional Class definition might have Extensions

•  The Functional Class Extensions might contain extensions from one or more extension Namespaces

•  An extension Namespaces might contain one or more extension Nodes

•  An extension Node might contain one or more Properties

•  An extension Node might contain one or more Child Nodes

•  A Class Library has a collection of Attribute Classes

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 247

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Use ISM

•  The collection of Attribute Classes might contain one or more Attribute Class definitions

•  An Attribute Class definition has a collection of pre-defined Properties

•  A Class Library definition might have Extensions

•  The Class Library Extensions might contain extensions from one or more extension Namespaces

•  An extension Namespaces might contain one or more extension Nodes

•  An extension Node might contain one or more Properties

The diagram shows a small selection of the full Class Library model in AVEVA ISM, just to give you an idea of how
the model works, as a background to understand Value expressions in a Permissible Grid configuration. The node
graph for Document, Physical and General classes are like the one showed for Functional classes in the diagram.

An example of a node value expression might look something like this:

<Columns headerStyle="TagHeader">
<Column geicl:id="ClassId" header="Functional Class Id" value="./@id" />
<Column geicl:id="ClassName" header="Functional Class Name" value="./@name"/>
</Columns>

During output of a Permissible Grid, AVEVA ISM iterates over the nodes in the target Class Library model (i.e. the
Attribute Classes, the Classes of specified concept and the Permissible Attributes of those Classes). A Value
expressions in the configuration is then interpreted in the context of the current node. The syntax example
above is an excerpt from the configuration on how to render properties from the Classes, down the left-hand
side of the Permissible Grid, hence these expressions are resolved in the context of a Class:

•  The "." (dot) at the start of the expression symbolizes the context, which in this case is the current Class

•  The "/" (forward slash) instructs to follow one of the connecting lines (4, 5 or 8 in the diagram above)

•  The "@" (at sign) instructs that the line to follow is for a property (i.e. line 4 in the diagram above)

•  The name following the "@" is the name of the property to pick (i.e. "id" and "name" respectively)

The result of these expressions is the value of property id, and name respectively, of the current Class.

Be aware that even though the diagram above shows extensions for a Class and the ClassLibrary node only, all
nodes in the AVEVA ISM Class Library model can potentially contain an Extensions node. The diagram above is
simplified for brevity.

Context nodes

The context node will vary, dependent on which section of the report configuration is in focus.

The Class Library Context

These report sections:

•  Variables

•  Setup

•  Styles

•  Page

•  Classes/Headers

executes in the context of the ClassLibrary node (see the diagram above). From this context, Value expressions

The report section Attributes/Headers executes in the context of an Attribute Class (see the diagram above),
while the section Attributes/Columns executes in the context of a Class Permissible Attribute. From this context,
node value expressions can access the collection of pre-defined properties and any Extensions of the Attribute
node (an Attribute Class and a Permissible Attribute has similar content and structure).

The Enumerations Context

The Enumerations context can be reached from the Class Library context by the path expression ./Enumerations.
From this context, node value expressions can access the items of the collection (i.e. Enumeration Lists) by an
indexing expression (e.g. ./Enumerations[myList]).

For an Enumeration List node, you can use node value expressions to access the collection of pre-defined
properties, any Extensions of the Enumeration List node and the colletion of Items.

Property Name

Description

id

name

description

aspect

sortOrder

sortKey

The Enumeration List Name

The Enumeration List Description

The Enumeration List Aspect tokens

The Enumeration List Sort Order as an integer value

The Enumeration List Sort Order formatted as a
sortable string value, suffixed by the Name property
and the Identifier property

The Enumeration Items Context

The Enumeration Items context can be reached from the Enumerations context by the path expression ./Items.
From this context, node value expressions can access the items of the collection (i.e. Enumeration List Items) by
an indexing expression (e.g. ./Enumerations[myList]/Items[myItem]).

For an Enumeration List Item node, you can use node value expressions to access the collection of pre-defined
properties, any Extensions of the Enumeration List Item node and any collection of sub-Items.

Property Name

Description

name

description

aspect

definitions

The MaturityLevels Context

The MaturityLevels context can be reached from the Class Library, Attribute and Permissible Attribute context by
the path expression ./MaturityLevels. From this context, node value expressions can access the items of the
collection (i.e. MaturityLevel) by an indexing expression (e.g. ./MaturityLevel[FEED]).

For a Maturity Level node, you can use node value expressions to access the collection of pre-defined properties
and any Extensions of the Maturity Level.

The LifeCycleTypes Context

The LifeCycleType context can be reached from the Class Library context by the path expression ./LifeCycleTypes.

properties, any Extensions of the LifeCycleType node and the colletion of MaturityLevels.

The LifeCycleType MaturityLevels Context

The LifeCycleType MaturityLevels context can be reached from the LifeCycleType context by the path expression

indexing expression (e.g. ./LifeCycleTypes[myLct]/MaturityLevels[myMaturityLevel]).

properties, any Extensions of the MaturityLevel node.

The Extensions context can be reached from any context by the path expression ./Extensions. From this context,
node value expressions can access the namespace of an extension by an indexing expression (e.g.
