Installation Wizard

This section outlines the steps involved in installing AVEVA Information Standards Manager.

Application Configuration

There are two ways of configuring the web sites:

•  As a separate site (for example, http://ism.aveva.com/)

•  As an application under an existing site (for example, http://www.aveva.com/aveva-ism/).

Use the following information to perform Web Configuration:

•  Site Name: Name of the site. This name will also be used in URLs for the application (in lower case with

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 352

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Administer ISM (on-prem)

hyphens instead of spaces).

•  Install Directory: Physical root directory where the application and its files will be installed.

Note: If you are upgrading from a previous version of AVEVA Information Standards Manager, specify a
different name for the folder in use (for the current version) to avoid any unintentional removal of optional
plugins.

•  Application Pool Configuration

•  User type: Configure the identity of the AVEVA Information Standards Manager process. You can either

use the default user, ApplicationPoolIdentity, or provide credentials for a custom user.

•  User name: User name for custom user.

•  Password: Password for custom user.

•  Install as separate site

•  Protocol: Select if you want to set up the site using HTTPS protocol.

•  Host name: Host/Domain of site. For example: ism.aveva.com.

•  Port: Port the site should run under.

•  SSL Certificate: Enabled if HTTP Secure is selected. Certificate to secure the site with. Lists all certificates

available in IIS.

•  Install as an application under existing site

•  Parent Site Name: Name of the parent site. Must exist, or the installation will fail.

Select Authentication Method

The following are the two methods of authentication for AVEVA Information Standards Manager:

•  Claims Based Authentication (Security Token Service): The Microsoft ADFS is an example of the STS.

•  Windows Authentication (Active Directory): This requires a comprehensive Active Directory configuration to
extract the information provided by claims. When using this authentication, AVEVA Information Standards
Manager Application Pool Identity needs to have access to perform queries against the configured Active
Directory server. See Web Configuration for configuration of AVEVA Information Standards Manager
Application Pool Identity.

Claims Based Authentication Configuration

Use the following information to perform STS Authentication Configuration:

Issuer url: The token service URL without the protocol (http/https) suffix.

Extract STS configuration from certificate: Select to extract the required information automatically.

•  Certificate: The certificate from which you want to extract the STS name and thumbprint. Lists all the

certificates available in IIS.

•  Provide STS configuration manually - Select to provide the required information manually. See the

documentation for your token service.

•  Name - The name of the token issuer.

•  Thumbprint - The thumbprint used to validate the signature in the token response.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 353

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Administer ISM (on-prem)

While using STS, see Security Token Service.

Windows Authentication Configuration

Use the following information to enter the correct Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP) details for AD
Authentication Configuration:

Note: This information is spread over two pages in the Installation Wizard.

Page 1 (of 2):

•  Server url: The LDAP URL, including the port number.

•  Use SSL: Select if you want to secure the connection with SSL.

•  Base DN: The base point from where to perform user/group search.

•  User object filter: The LDAP filter to use when searching for user objects.

•  Group object filter: The LDAP filter to use when searching for group objects.

•  Global Property Mapping

•  Distinguished name: The name of the LDAP User and Group attribute that contain the object

distinguished name (DN).

•  User Property Mapping

•  Organisation: The name of the LDAP User attribute that will populate the Organization claim.

•  User name: The name of the LDAP User attribute that represents the user name.

•  Display name: The name of the LDAP User attribute that will populate the DisplayName claim.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 354

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Administer ISM (on-prem)

•  Email: The name of the LDAP User attribute that will populate the E-mail claim.

•  Group Property Mapping

•  Group members: The name of the LDAP Group attribute that references the group member

distinguishedNames (DN).

Page 2 (of 2):

•  Reader Capability, Author Capability, Creator Capability and Administrator Capability

•  Distinguished group name: you can add one or more groups to each capability. Use the group's

distinguished name.

•  Everyone - select this option to give all users, no matter what group, access to this capability.

Note: Administrator Capability does not support use of "Everyone".

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 355

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Administer ISM (on-prem)

See Security Token Service (STS) for details on the different types of user capabilities.

Database Configuration

AVEVA Information Standards Manager uses a database to store its class libraries. The installation tool can be
used to test the connection and user roles.

Notes: If you are upgrading from a previous version of AVEVA Information Standards Manager, the values
provided in this configuration page must match those used for the initial install.

Use the following information to help you enter the correct details for the Database Configuration step:

•  SQL Server - Hostname for the machine where the SQL Server is hosted.

•  Database name - The name of the database.

•  Multiple organizations - Select this check box if you only want to see standards (Class Libraries) for your
organizations, otherwise you will be able to see all standards (class libraries) regardless of organization.

•  Create or Update database with latest script: Select this check box to let the system update the database
scripts for you. You can manually update the database script by clearing the selection for this check box.
When this check box is cleared, both the Initialize and Update options appear disabled.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 356

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Administer ISM (on-prem)

•  Database Server Credentials:

Initialize: This option is enabled only when you select the Create or Update database with latest script
check box. Select this option if you wish to create a database.

Update: This option is enabled only when you select the Create or Update database with latest script
check box. Select this option if you wish to update a database:

Current Version: Use this to select the currently installed version.

Notes: The updates are incremental. The dropdown lists only those releases for which there are
database updates.

Target Version: This read-only field displays the version of the installer.

Use Windows credentials: Select this check box if you wish the system to use your Windows credentials.
If you select this check box, the Server username and Server password field appear disabled.

Server username: If enabled, use this field to enter the username.

Note: The user should have dbcreator Server Role assigned.

Server password: If enabled, use this field to enter the password.

Recommendation: We need to maintain a separation of privileges between machine administrators and
SQL server administrators.

•  ISM User Details:

•  Administrator - Used for operations and procedures that require a higher level of security clearance.

•  User - Used for all standard operations and procedures.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 357

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Administer ISM (on-prem)

Install the application

The final step installs the application. The installer checks the prerequisite frameworks and validates the
configurations. If there is anything missing, the installation will stop and give an error message.

Note: The application will not work if the authentication method or the license server are unavailable or
incorrectly configured.

Configuration and Security Settings

This section gives more detail about setting up some of the requirements.

Installer-based Creation of SQL Server Database

data, DO NOT create a new database, skip this section and go to Upgrading an Existing Installation.

The installer creates an empty database and set up two database users, one with and the other without the
administrator privileges. The installer also maps the users to the database by assigning respective privileges and

You can manually perform the process based on the following information:

Step 1 - To create a new SQL Server database:

1.  Log on to your Database Server machine and open SQL Server Management Studio.

2.

In Object Explorer, expand an instance of the SQL Server Database Engine.

3.  Right-click Databases, then click New Database.

4.

In the Database window, type ISM or another suitable Database Name.

5.  Select an Owner if you do not want to use the default.

6.  For the Logical Name of the primary data file, type ISM.

7.  For the Logical Name of the transaction log file, type ISM_log, then click OK.

Note: Make sure to note the Database name, as you will need to use the same name later.

For AVEVA Information Standards Manager to fully manage the database, you need to create two kinds of users:

•  A standard user - Used for all standard operations and procedures.

Note: For information on how to create a database, see Step 1 of Manual Creation of SQL Server Database.

3.  To grant permission to administrator users, find and replace three occurrences of '$(ismAdministrator)'
with a username that has administrator privilege (See Notes). To grant permission to standard users, find
and replace two occurrences of '$(ismUser)' with a username that has standard user privilege (See Notes).
For example, replace ['+$(ismAdministrator)+'] with ['+'ISM_Admin'+'], and ['+$(ismUser)+']
with ['+'ISM_User'+'].

Notes:

•  For information on how to create an administrator user, see Step 3 of Manual Creation of SQL Server

Database.

•  For information on how to create a standard user, see Step 2 of Manual Creation of SQL Server Database.

4.  Run this script.

Security Token Service (STS)

The Security Token Service (STS) needs to provide the following claims for users:

1.  Capability

URI: http://schemas.aveva.com/ism/2014/01/claims/capability
Can be one of these values:

•  Reader

•  Users with this value will have read only access to AVEVA Information Standards Manager.

•  They are allowed to upload Class Libraries.

•  They are not allowed to save Class Libraries to Catalogues.

•  Author

•  Users with this value will have read and write access to AVEVA Information Standards Manager.

•  They will have all capabilities of Readers.

•  They are allowed to lock existing standards to make changes.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 365

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Administer ISM (on-prem)

•  They are not allowed to save Class Libraries to Catalogues.

•  Creator

•  Users with this value will have read, write and create access to AVEVA Information Standards Manager.

•  They will have all capabilities of Readers and Authors.

•  They are allowed to create and save Class Libraries to Catalogue.

•  Administrator

•  Users with this value will have read, write and create access to AVEVA Information Standards Manager.

•  They will have all capabilities of Readers, Authors and Creators.

•  They are allowed to Unlock a Class Library on behalf of another User.

•  They are allowed to permanently delete a Class Library, as long as conditions are met.

1.  Organization

URI: http://schemas.aveva.com/ism/2014/01/claims/organisation
The organization name of the User. Users will only have access to Standards owned by this Organization. All
users within an organization must have the same organization name.

2.  Email address

URI: http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/emailaddress
The email address of the user, the email address must be unique for all users of AVEVA Information
Standards Manager.

3.  Name identifier

URI: http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/nameidentifier
The unique name (user name) of the user. This must be unique within an organization.

4.  Name

URI: http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/name
The display name of the user.

Add HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS)

HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) allows web servers to declare that any web browser should only use an
HTTPS connection to interact with it. This helps in securing the website against protocol downgrade attacks and
cookie hijacking. It is a recommended configuration for any HTTPS installation of AVEVA Information Standards
Manager.

Important: This must not be done for HTTP installations.

To enable this header for each request, add the following configuration to the <customHeaders> node under
<system.webServer> of your web.config file:

<httpProtocol>
<customHeaders>
<add name="Strict-Transport-Security" value="max-age=31536000"/>
</customHeaders>
</httpProtocol>

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 366

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Administer ISM (on-prem)

Protection Against Banner Grabbing

Banner grabbing is used to gain information or access to any remote systems running on a network and their
services that may run on open ports. To protect against this attack, it is recommended that you perform the
following operations:

•  Remove version headers from IIS

•  Remove 'X-Powered-By' header from IIS

Remove Version Headers from IIS

The IIS sends the following headers for each request:

•  Server - Specifies the IIS server version.

•  X-AspNet-Version - Specifies the version of ASP.NET framework used.

It is recommended to remove identifiers from software, such as application servers and web servers, to limit the
amount of information obtained by a potentially hostile party. The applications running on ASP.NET using IIS have
such identifiers turned ON by default. It is recommended that these headers be removed from the response IIS
generates for both non-secure (HTTP) and secure (HTTPS) installations.

To rewrite the values for these headers:

1.

Install the compatible version of the URL Rewrite module for IIS. For information, refer to
https://www.iis.net/downloads/microsoft/url-rewrite.

2.  Open <ISM Installation path>/Client.

3.  Open web.config in a text editor.

4.

In the <system.webServer> section, paste the following:

<rewrite>
<outboundRules>
<rule name="Remove Server header">
<match serverVariable="RESPONSE_Server" pattern=".+" />
<action type="Rewrite" value="" />
</rule>

<rule name="Remove X-ASPNet-Version header">
<match serverVariable="RESPONSE_X_ASPNet_Version" pattern=".+" />
<action type="Rewrite" value="" />
</rule>
</outboundRules>
</rewrite>

5.  Reset IIS.

The header can also be removed instead of being rewritten. This may be subject to the server setup and
configuration requirements and at the discretion of your system administrator. Use UrlScan 3.1 to remove the
headers completely. For information, refer to http://www.iis.net/learn/extensions/working-with-urlscan/urlscan-
overview. After installing and configuring the tool, set the RemoveServerHeader and AllowDotInPath option to
1 in the URLScan.ini file.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 367

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Administer ISM (on-prem)

Remove 'X-Powered-By' header from IIS

To remove 'X-Powered-By' headers:

1.  Open <ISM Installation path>/Client.

2.  Open web.config in a text editor.

3.

In the <system.webServer> section, paste the following:

<httpProtocol>
<customHeaders>
<remove name="X-Powered-By" />
</customHeaders>
</httpProtocol>

Upgrading an Existing Installation

To upgrade AVEVA Information Standards Manager from a previous version:

1.  Upgrade the AVEVA Information Standards Manager database to the latest version.

2.  Complete the Installation Wizard, noting the following:

•  AVEVA Information Standards Manager can be extended with plugins that are not part of the latest

installer. You must manage these plugins independently because they and released by AVEVA outside of
the AVEVA Information Standards Manager release schedule.

•  When you install the latest version of AVEVA Information Standards Manager in the same folder location
as that of a previous version, the existing plugin folders are replaced with their respective latest versions.
However, when a plugin is no longer available for installation, the existing plugin is retained in the
installation folder. If you are installing in a different folder location, you need to manually copy the
plugins that are not part of the latest installer.

•  When you re-install AVEVA Information Standards Manager from the same user account, the last used

configuration settings are pre-populated as suggestions.

Backing up an Existing Installation

Before performing a database upgrade, it is strongly recommended that you make a copy of the current
database and ensure that the backed-up data is not affected by the new installation. In addition, you should
install AVEVA Information Standards Manager in a new folder, therefore preventing the existing installation from
being overridden.

Upgrade the SQL Server Database from 2.0 to 2.1

The installer automatically updates the existing database. Alternatively, you can manually update the database
using the procedure that follows.

To upgrade the SQL Server Database:

1.  Log on to your Database Server machine and open SQL Server Management Studio.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

2.  Browse to the folder where the AVEVA Information Standards Manager Installer is located.

3.  Run AVEVA ISM v2.0.0.0-to-v2.1.0.0 - MS SQL Server Upgrade.sql. Ensure that you are running the

following referential script in the right database context: use <database name>.

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Administer ISM (on-prem)

Troubleshooting

The following section provides troubleshooting information.

Users Not Authorised or Missing Claims

After AVEVA Information Standards Manager is installed and the authentication method has been configured,
some users may find that they are not authorised to use the product even though they are able to authenticate
successfully. This may be caused by the STS not being correctly configured. Users will not be authorised unless all
the required claims listed in the above section are given a value.

Note: Microsoft ADFS does not provide claims with empty values. A message is logged in the Application section
of the Windows Event Log with details on the missing claims.

ID3206 Exception

If you install AVEVA Information Standards Manager as an application under existing site:

http://<hostname>/aveva-ism

you will need to access it with a URL containing a trailing slash:

http://<hostname>/aveva-ism/

If your user is not authenticated and you do not use the trailing slash, you will get the following exception:

ID3206: A SignInResponse message may only redirect within the current web application:
'/aveva-ism' is not allowed.

Description: An unhandled exception occurred during the execution of the current web
request. Please review the stack trace for more information about the error and where it
originated in the code.

Exception Details: Microsoft.IdentityModel.Protocols.FederationException: ID3206: A
SignInResponse message may only redirect within the current web application: '/aveva-ism'
is not allowed.

This is a weakness in Windows Identity Framework (WIF). There are currently no workarounds for this issue.

Unable to Read Encrypted DB Configuration

The database section in the Web.config is encrypted so that the the services/api do not have passwords in clear
text. If your site is unable to decrypt the information in the Web.config file, this may be because the Application
Pool Identity lacks access to the encryption key used to encryption and decryption. Run the command below and
replace the "DOMAIN\myuserid" with your Application Pool Identity:

aspnet_regiis -pa "NetFrameworkConfigurationKey" "DOMAIN\myuserid"

For information, refer to http://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/vstudio/dtkwfdky(v=vs.100).aspx.

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Page 370

AVEVA™ Information Standards Manager
Administer ISM (on-prem)

Could Not Obtain a Valid License

If the following error is seen with a valid license, then in IIS, ensure Enable 32-bit applications is set to False
under Application Pools > <site name> > Advanced Settings.

Please complete the Administration Exercises

Tom’s Background:
Tom has been at Diagonals for nearly 30 years. He started as a Junior Technician after
leaving school and worked his way up to engineer where he had a successful career for
many years. However, when computers first started making an appearance in the industry
Tom discovered his real niche and has never looked back. He still sees himself as an
engineer though, and prides himself on understanding these aspects of the job too.
Tom’s main job is to keep the system running and up to date. He has a lot of involvement
when new systems are first installed and when new projects start. At other times he makes
sure that information is loaded into the system correctly and provides support and in-
house-training to other employees.

User Profile:
As Tom was involved in the implementation of AVEVA NET at Diagonals he is familiar with
the installation. As such he is very comfortable using the Admin Tool and Import
Controller to check information in the system on the behalf of others. However most of
the time he uses the Admin Tool to update user information/permissions or privileges and
the Import Controller to update the latest information when necessary.

ABOUT AVEVA

AVEVA is a world leader in industrial software, providing engineering and operational solutions across multiple industries,
including oil and gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power and utilities, marine, renewables, and food and beverage. Our
agnostic and open architecture helps organizations design, build, operate, maintain and optimize the complete lifecycle
of complex industrial assets, from production plants and offshore platforms to manufactured consumer goods.

Over 20,000 enterprises in over 100 countries rely on AVEVA to help them deliver life’s essentials: safe and reliable
energy, food, medicines, infrastructure and more. By connecting people with trusted information and AI-enriched
insights, AVEVA enables teams to engineer efficiently and optimize operations, driving growth and sustainability.

Named as one of the world’s most innovative companies, AVEVA supports customers with open solutions and the
expertise of more than 6,400 employees, 5,000 partners and 5,700 certified developers. The company is headquartered
in Cambridge, UK.

Learn more at www.aveva.com

ABOUT AVEVA
AVEVA is a global leader in engineering and industrial software driving digital transformation across
the entire asset and operational life cycle of capital-intensive industries.

The company’s engineering, planning and operations, asset performance, and monitoring and
control solutions deliver proven results to over 16,000 customers across the globe. Its customers are
supported by the largest industrial software ecosystem, including 4,200 partners and 5,700 certified
developers. AVEVA is headquartered in Cambridge, UK, with over 4,400 employees at 80 locations in
over 40 countries.

Slide 3

...Engineering Information As It Should Be!

Global view

A Global group off companies and consultants working to improve Engineering
Information Management Capture, Assure and Share always having the End in Mind.
Objectives:

➢ Fast and Easy to use, a SaaS Solution to lower cost, improve efficiency, minimise risk

and increase Safety

➢ Easy in – Easy out, reducing employees frustration
➢ Master Tag register for controlling all existing and future Facilities

➢

Live Registers and Reports/Dashboarding

➢ Flexible Interoperable – controlled by business driven rules (Class Libraries)
➢ Futureproof – Bring all standards and process together MUST BE Software agnostic.
➢ Rigorous Change Management and Full Audit Trial History
➢ Digital Assets and Physical Assets need to be Aligned
➢ Alignment with Document control systems Live Link, Documentum, SP Foundation,

ProjectWise, SharePoint, Vault, BIM360 Docs, etc.

➢ Alignment with operational solutions SAP, Maximo, Predix etc.

➢

3D viewing and redline mark-up and GIS Location

➢ Must Learn from Providing proven best practices from cross industries and applying
industry standards with in the Mining & Metals, Power, Water & Waste Water and
Construction.

Houston
Kuala Lumpur
United Kingdom

Brisbane, Queensland

Perth, Western Australia

JHB South Africa

Copyright © 2016 Datum360 Limited
All rights reserved

Slide 4

...Engineering Information As It Should Be!

Top challenges keeping O/O , Contractor and
EPC  awake at night.

Copyright © 2016 Datum360 Limited
All rights reserved

Slide 5

...Engineering Information As It Should Be!

Things to look out for “Lesson Learned”

In-House customisation “No” “No”
No SQL ….Time lost when a rebuild is require.
Always use multiple vendors solutions increasing the clients you
need to service.
What ever you Use, needs to be Software agnostics, flexible and
configurable “not customised". Off the self is best…………..for
future upgrades.
Ultimately the Design and Development of and EDW/ BIMDW
needs to keep the Future in Mind.
Maintain a federated approach , (like google)
Design to support the integrations of Multiple Data sources
Design using ETL functionality.(Extract Transmit & Load) Standard
process.

Copyright © 2016 Datum360 Limited
All rights reserved

Slide 6

...Engineering Information As It Should Be!

Items that may Help

Question you need to ask yourself?

What are you trying to accomplish and why?
To make this happen, what do you need to do and when do you
need to do it?
How can you accomplish only what your design needs you to do?
The process needs to be simple and repeatable by others to be
successful. ?
Who on your team will be responsible for doing what tasks
Always Design your system ,Make sure you have the “End in mind”
future-proof your solution

Copyright © 2016 Datum360 Limited
All rights reserved

Slide 7

...Engineering Information As It Should Be!

Items that may Help

What is a Class Library Definition .

A class library is a collection of prewritten classes or coded templates or
Rules that may be used to develop and application.
Typically use in engineering to control standards for the Digital reuse on
project, Operations, and Maintenance.

Custodians need to take responsibility for the Class Library, Standard
and processes.
Use a unstructured Database with a class Library….will save you time
Alignment of Digital and Physical assets.
Concurrent Handover using SaaS. Always keep the End in mind….

Copyright © 2016 Datum360 Limited
All rights reserved

Melinda Hemstead, Data Consultant

March 2021 | Version 1.3

Naming and Numbering Templates (ISM N&N Templates)

Naming and Numbering Templates (ISM N&N Templates)

Validation - Naming templates
applicable for Validation only can then
be used to validate the names of existing
objects
Allocation - allocating names to new
objects

You can chose to assign BOTH Validation
and Allocation to your Naming Template
therefore it can be used for both
Validating of existing objects and
Allocating of new objects

Naming and Numbering Templates (ISM N&N Templates)

Validation - Naming templates
You can chose to assign BOTH Validation
applicable for Validation only can then
and Allocation to your Naming Template
be used to validate the names of existing
therefore it can be used for both
objects
Validating of existing objects and
Allocation - allocating names to new
Allocating of new objects
objects

Naming and Numbering Elements (N&N Elements)

Create an ID, Name and Description for each of the elements that make up your Tag
Numbers.
Add in the Source of that information – this is an Attribute for your Element and will
also be required to be added into your Attributes tab.
Advise if the Element is a mandatory requirement or if it is optional using a True and
False indicator

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Naming and Numbering Elements (N&N Elements)

Add in any hyphens/fullstops/slashes
that are part of your Tag format into
the Prefix and Suffix columns (use
only 1 column per element)

Add the minimum and maximum
number of expected characters

Add in a 0 into the Padding Character
area unless you want to pad out an
element with a specific character.

Add in the Regular Expression for
your Element

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Naming and Numbering Elements (N&N Elements)

Add in any hard codes that are part of your
Tag Element into the FixedValue column.

Add the sort order of the Elements for the
Tag Format – important to ensure the
Elements are entered into ISM into the
correct format.

Use the Obsolete Column to advise any
Element that becomes obsolete in the
future.

Add in a specific Aspect in the format:
Attributes.’Element Attribute Name’
Eg – Attributes.VATTR-TRAIN

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Enumeration List for Tag Elements

Create Enumeration lists for each of
the Naming Elements as indicated by
your N&N Specification.

The header of your list needs to be
assigned to the appropriate Attribute

Create the list with a header row and
then follow the format as shown
below

MECHANICAL TAG FUNCTION|MBS

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Naming Elements into Attributes Tab

© 2019 AVEVA Group plc and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

On the Attributes tab complete the
columns as shown (same columns as you
would complete for adding other
Attributes) by bringing your ‘Source’
Attribute from the Naming Elements tab
Add any Enumeration Lists that apply to
any of the Naming Elements as depicted
in your N&N Specification

Elements your only

order field so ISM holds the

Number string

DNS

EDW

EPC

IHS

Document Numbering Specification

Engineering Data Warehouse. A managed environment for
Operations handover data that provides a single source of
structured engineering data for maintainable and non-maintainable
equipment, instrumentation and piping and other engineering
discipline data in support of the O&M phase of the facility.

EPC
Engineering Procurement and Construction. The EPCs
execute all or part of the design, engineering, procurement,
construction, commissioning or management of a project or O&M
of a facility.

In the context of this document EPCs also refers to their Suppliers,
Vendors, OEM’s and fabricators and the EPCs assumes
responsibility for ensuring their handover information conforms to
the IHS.

Information Handover Specification –Detailed handover
requirements defining the Owners requirements for engineering
and Operations information and the schedule for its handover.

ISO14224

Petroleum, petrochemical and natural gas industries – Collection
and exchange of reliability and maintenance data for equipment

ISO15926 Pt4

Industrial automation systems and integration. Integration of life-
cycle data for process plants including oil and gas production
facilities, Part 4: Initial reference data

MoC

PISTEP

Management of Change

Process Industries STEP Consortium

POSC/CAESAR

Petrotechnical Open Software Consortium

UoM

USPI

Units of Measurement - Units assigned to Attribute Properties, e.g.
mm, kg/m3, ft,  etc.

Uitgebreid Samenwerkingsverband Processindustrie. Nederland
Not for profit organisation to develop, maintain and promote the
use of international standards and best practices for product and
plant life cycle information. AVEVA is a reviewing member and
contributor to USPI

2.3  Glossary

Term

Attribute

Description

A named item of data which is relevant to the class to which it’s
Attribute Set may be allocated. For example: Design Pressure, Design
Temperature, etc.

Issued

Page 4 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

Attribute Set

A named set of Attributes relevant to an engineering class, e.g.
‘Design conditions’, ‘Process Conditions’ etc. Attribute Sets will be
allocated to relevant classes as required.

Class

Class Alias

Document

Facility

Library

List of Values

List value

Property

System

Tagged Item

Term used to classify plant equipment, an engineering object of
significance; an entity; e.g. Pump, System, Document, Drawing, etc.

An alternative name for a class; Used to identify class information
being supplied by third parties. Ideally any third parties will have
been mandated to use the same Class Library as part of their
contract.

Information (Printed or Electronic). Reports, Specifications, Data
sheets and Drawings

A Facility (also known as plant or platform) is a distinctly identifiable
piece of infrastructure that is part of the whole project (or field, or
site). Each Facility (if there is more than one) is likely to be
geographically distinct or a platform
A collection of Classes for a given target customer’s application(s)
delivered as a Class Hierarchy.
Some Attributes may have to be present in a List of Values. This
information must therefore be a Property of the Attribute. There are
two types of List, static and dynamic. Data for static lists may be
defined and managed in the Class Library.
The actual values for Lists of Values can be managed lest they too
must be loaded into the target application. This is possible for static
lists only.
A named item of data defining an Attribute. These are fixed and
include such things as mnemonic, name, description, data type,
default value, units type, etc.
Systems are the breakdown of a Facility into functional units that
perform a specific task. This is typically a two-level breakdown with
major functional systems being split into smaller components. The
association between the Facility and the System / Sub-System is
represented by an association called ‘is part of ‘
A Tagged Item represents the functional task or role of a piece of
equipment and represents the location of the equipment and is the
smallest breakdown of equipment that the Owner chooses to
identify. A tag number remains the same throughout the life of the
plant that appears on P&IDs, 3D models, schedules, line lists etc.

Issued

Page 5 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

3  Purpose
The potential risk of poor quality data at handover from the EPC’s to the Owner
represents considerable civil and operational risk to both parties. Poor quality
information is caused by a variety of reasons, not least the lack of consistency due to
an entropic network of data created by a multitude of engineering contractors using a
variety of different source applications.

The adoption of a Class Library4 considerably reduces the risk of poor quality data as it
provides the means to validate the handover information against the Owners
information standard to determine the ‘known state’ of handover information. The
Owners Information Standard communicates the detailed requirements and criteria in
support of the Owners governance of data integrity.

This document describes the AVEVA Information Standards Manager (AVEVA ISM)
version 2.2 developed Class Library4 and its underlying principles and concepts relating
to engineering Classes, Attributes, Units of Measure Associativity and Disciplines.

The Class Library4, together with the Owners handover documentation enables the
capture of a clearly and concisely defined standard to ensure that the quality and
completeness of EPC handover information conforms to the Owners information
standard in support of the following lifecycles including:

  Design
  Engineering
  Procurement
  Fabrication and Assembly
  Commissioning
  Operations and Maintenance, and
  Decommissioning

The Class Library4 references the ISO 15926 RDL, CFIHOS and includes additional
enhancements based on industry best practice gathered from client engagements and
product implementations.

Issued

Page 6 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

4  Definition of a Class Library?
A Class Library4 provides a means to consistently define the information requirements
for every type of engineering object including their taxonomies and provide the
foundation to deliver trusted data governance in the following ways:





It provides an information standard by which to measure the compliance,
completeness and consistency of the information provided against a given
engineering object such as a Pump, Valve or Document
It simplifies data management and improves alignment with industry standards,
Owners requirements and statutory bodies’ safety requirements

  Along with the requisite validation rules it provides a means by which to

measure data quality including validation of the Units of Measure.
It provides rules which define the different types of data, including associations



The project dependency on a fit for purpose Class Library4 has increased as the
handover of project information from the EPCs to the Owner has evolved from the
traditional document centric model to the data centric model that exists now.

4.1  Benefits of a Class Library
The benefits introduced by the adoption of a Class Library4 to the project are not only
related to the validation of handover information, it also supports the following
corporate and strategic programmes and system dependencies:

Corporate

•  Operational excellence programmes
•  Asset data quality benchmarking
•  Corporate level data systems implementations
•  License to Operate (data compliance)
•  Regional programmes (spares rationalisation)
•  Common corporate language

Project Asset Level

•  Definition of criteria in the Information Handover Specification
•  Handover of data during project phases
•  Efficiency during project phases
•
•  License to Operate (Regulatory Compliance)
•  Reduces data and document management overhead
•  Maintenance and Inspection programmes

Identifies pre-requisites for pre-operations and early operations activities

Business System Level

•  Data transfer and configuration specification
•  Data completeness and readiness reports
•  System implementation time
•  System functionality

Issued

Page 7 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

4.2  Class Library Best Practice
The following guidelines and suggestions are based on industry best practice and
lessons learnt on a number of Class Library4 developments and implementations, both
internal and external. The principles of the these recommendations have been
incorporated into the Class Library4 to provide a fit for purpose dataset supported by
the associated handover document suite on which the Owners handover standard can
be based.

4.2.1  General Recommendations

  Class Library ID to be Alpha numeric, i.e. AVEVA_0001 to represent the Class



Library with a corresponding Name i.e. AVEVA Corporate Class Library.
 Unique ID’s to be Alpha numeric, i.e. ‘FC_0001’ to represent Functional Class
and ‘FA_0001’ to represent Functional Attribute objects. The use of Class or
attribute names should be avoided as it complicates the unique logic.
  Full Descriptions of Classes, Attributes and UOMs to be added to the Class

Library4 for completeness and to avoid ambiguity

  Where possible remove any duplicate classes
  Create a well thought out parent child relationships to ensure a well-structured

hierarchy.

  Consider an inheritance model for attributes. Create core attributes at the top of

hierarchal tree which will be inherited by all ‘leaf end’ classes.
  Consider grouping UoMs to a measure class i.e. Length, ‘Pressure’,

‘Temperature’, ‘Flow’ etc., this will simplify subsequent maintenance of the
Class Library4

  Adopt a text style that will aid visual recognition, such as all class and attribute
names in separate font types i.e. all Capitals for Classes and Proper (Upper and
Lower) for attributes as below:

o  Classes in UPPERCASE; ‘ANALYSIS – ALARM HIGH’
o  Attributes in Proper; ‘Hazardous Area Classification (Required)’

  The following conventions have been adopted for the Class Library4

‘Proper text’ has been used throughout

o
o  Core Classes (top level classes) are prefixed with ‘CC_’
o  Document Classes are prefixed with ‘DCC_’
o  Functional Classes are prefixed with ‘FC_’
o  General Classes are prefixed with ‘GC_’
o  Lifecycle Classes are prefixed with ‘LC_’
o  Physical Classes are prefixed with ‘PC_’
o  Document Attributes are prefixed with ‘DA_’
o  Functional Attributes are prefixed with ‘FA_’
o  General Attribute are prefixed with ‘GA_’
o  Maturity Level Attributes are prefixed with ‘ML_’
o  Physical Attributes are prefixed with ‘PA_’
o  Tag Attributes are prefixed with ‘TA_’
o  Units of Measure Classes use the class ‘name’ i.e WEIGHT and
o  Unit of Measure units use the permissible units name i.e. kg, g, mg, cg,

dig, hg etc.

o  Naming and Numbering Element for ENS Tag items should be prefixed

with a ‘EI’

Issued

Page 8 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

o  Naming and Numbering Element for DNS Document items should be

prefixed with a ‘DI’

o  Naming and Numbering Element for ENS Tag collections should be

prefixed with a ‘EC’

o  Naming and Numbering Element for DNS Document collections should be

prefixed with a ‘DC’

  Provide a suitable Class to Document association mapping to aid identification

of missing documents to a Tag.

Note: When mapping a Corporate Standard Class Library to the CFIHOS standard, it
will be necessary to adopt the CFIHOS ID to ensure inheritance of the CFIHOS
information including ISO 15926 – Part 4 mapping etc.

4.2.2  Best Practice - Classes

  Ensure that Classes are mapped to the Equipment Naming Specication1
  Classes are logically structured in hierarchies or taxonomies such as a Discipline
  Class names are clear and concise.
  Classes are related to the Functional and Physical model
  Avoid ambiguities or unintentional duplication of the Function coding, an

example of which is:

o  FE (Flow Element)
o  FE (Fire Extinguisher)

  Names consistent to the appropriate standard i.e. ISO 15926 part 4
  Adoption of unique ID’s
  Understand operational system dependencies and consider mapping to ISO

14224 or any CMMS application (SAP, Maximo)
  All Classes are mapped to Engineering Reports
  Determine tagging requirements for ‘Hard’ and ‘Soft’ tags, consider tagging
requirements for Requisition’s, Purchase Order’s, Transmittals, Penetrations,
Tie-In’s, Supports, Modules, Piping Spools, Engineering Work Packs,
Construction Work Packs etc.?

4.2.3  Best practice - Attributes

  Attribute names are clear and concise and based on industry standard

terminology

  Avoid including Attributes that are ambiguous or there is doubt to their
usefulness, attributes (and Classes) should be relevant and useful to the
discipline engineer and have a measurable benefit to Design or Operations

  Create set of core attributes which are standard to all Equipment items
  Attribute are associated to the correct classes avoiding ‘Not Applicable’ where

possible

  Ensure validation of data type can be overridden to allow for text string in real

numbers

  Consider splitting Min & Max attributes into two separate objects
  Try to group attribute by context i.e. Safety, Process Conditions, and

Dimension…
Identify any Rules in validating attribute Values i.e. lookup list etc.


  Consider a set of Maturity code for each Attribute. Mandatory, Optional, Not

Applicable

Issued

Page 9 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

4.2.4  Best Practice - UoM

  UoM names are clear and concise and based on industry standard i.e. ISO 31,

ISO 80000 etc.

  Ensure standard font for all square and cubes (m2/m2, m3/m3)
  Ensure standard font for all Greek characters
  Try to group UoM by measured values i.e. ‘Pressure’, ‘Temperature’, ‘Flow’ etc.
  Ensure UoM which is current and not obsolete or out of date.


Identify those Attributes having multiple measure classes and manage
accordingly in the Class Library4; typical attribute are

o  Calibrated Ranges
o  Set points
o  Capacities



Identification of the attributes that have unit less values such as:

o  Specific gravity (SG)
o  Distinguish between Ratios and Percentages and their correct application

4.2.5  Best Practice - Associations



Identify what associations are useful in a Class Library4

o  Class to Document
o  Class to Class (Class-System, Class-Area, Class-Loop, Tag-Tag (3d model))
o  Class - Attribute



If adopting a Functional / Physical model ensure that associations between
classes are correct and try to avoid duplication

  Establish if inheritance of attributes is to be used and model the hierarchy

according to a consistent rule.

  Ensure the link between the maintenance model and the Class is established

(ISO 15926 and ISO 14224 (future))

  Rules for the instantiation of ‘leaf end’ classes can be created in the form of

class to class associations. Typical examples include:

o  1 to many Associations

  Area - Tagged Item
  System - Tagged Item
  Loop - Instrument Tags
  Line - Line Section
  Cables -  Tagged Items
  Lines Section - Tagged Items (to /from)

  Commissioning Packs - Tagged Items

Junction Box - Instrument Tags and Electrical Tags

o  1 to 1Associations

  Valve – Actuator
  Pump - Motor

5  What defines a Class Library?
In addition to the standards on which a Class Library4 is based the mechanism or
objects used to classify information is achieved with 5 basic elements, these are:

  Classes

o  Functional and Physical Tag classes
o  General Classes (i.e. Plant/Area/Unit hierarchy or System/Sub-System

hierarchy

o  Document Classes

  Attributes

o  Functional and Physical attributes

  Units of Measure (UoM)

o  Permissible Units of Measure

  Disciplines

o  Logical taxonomy for Tag Classes

  Associations

o  Association between Classes, Attributes and UoM
o  Provides a foundation for a project wide Mock

5.1  Classes
A class is a thing of significance in an application, such as a Pump, Valve, Document
and classes may be broken down by ‘Specialisation’ of the Class thus forming a
hierarchy. For example, a class of ‘Valve’ is too simplistic for the number and functions
of Valves in a plant, it is therefore common to have a specific Class such as ‘Control
Valve’ which in turn may have a further specialisation of ‘Flow Valve’ denoting the
actual type of valve to be managed.

The function of the valve will also determine the owning Discipline for the Class as
illustrated below.

Equipment-Piping

Instrument Device

Discipline

Manual
Valve

Ball
Valve

Control
Valve

Flow
Valve

Safety
Valve

Deluge
Valve

Parent Class

Specialisation

The same is true for Document Classes where a Document can have specialisations
such as Design Document, As Built Design Document etc.

Issued

Page 11 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

5.1.1  Functional Classes

The purpose of the Functional Class Object is to store the Tag Number and the
functional requirements of the Tag that define the “design intent” of the equipment
item.

5.1.2  Physical Classes

The purpose of the Physical Class Object stores the physical attributes i.e. the
characteristics of the device used to fulfil the (functional) design requirements, for
example:

  Type
  Weight
  Dimensions / Size of the Equipment

Physical data is normally available once the Supplier or Original Equipment
Manufacturer (OEM) is identified.  This would normally include, but not limited to:

  The Make and Model Number of a tagged item are key pieces of data and many

different tagged items on a project will utilize the same device and it is important
to know this.

  The Serial Number of an item links the tagged item (Functional object) to the

installed item.

  A final definitive Asset Tag shall be generated when the CMMS system is

operational.

The physical piece of plant is uniquely identified in the real world by a Serial Number
assigned by the manufacturer. Serial Numbers define the physical item performing the
function of the Tagged Item and are often related back to the Model Number that they
are an example of.

The two objects are closely related and typically modelled separately, the relationship
between a Serial Number and Tagged Number can be addressed as:

  Tag Number  Association  Serial Number

The Model Number is a type of standard equipment item that can be purchased from a
Manufacturer and can be common across Tagged Items e.g. Foxboro 841GM-BI1-C
Pressure Indicating Transmitter can be associated to many PT functional tags.

The relationship between the Tagged Item (functional) and the Model Number
(physical) can be addressed as:




Model Number  Association  Tagged Number
Model number  Association  Serial Number

A more detailed view of a Functional / Physical content and relationships for a Flow
Transmitter and the relationship types including document associations is shown
below:

KBA-IE-210-005

5.1.2.1  Extending the Functional / Physical Model

Extending the functional / Physical model to incorporate the mapping of the ISO14224
failure classes and data to the functional classes to support the capture of operational
data is planned for a future releases of the Class Library4

5.2  Document Classes
The distinction between Document Classes is important because they provide an
indication of the following important information:

  The Classification of whether the Document is a ‘Supplier Document’ (Physical)

or a ‘Design Document’ (Functional)

  Purpose of the Document; Design, Procurement, Maintenance etc.
  The Type of Document; a P&ID prescribes the content of the Document and is a
good indicator of the document containing tagged items and Tag numbers,
including to/From relationships to other documents
Identifies the Document as being (R) Required, (P) Preferred, (O) Optional or not
required for Operations



The tagged items full dataset is the combination of functional, physical and document
data requirements.

5.3  Attributes
Classes have attributes and attributes can be grouped into Attribute Sets and an
Attribute Set may be a generic grouping of Attributes common to all or several classes
of engineering equipment. For example:

Core Attributes include but not limited to the following:

  Service Description

Issued

Page 13 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

  Sub System
  System
  Tag Criticality Value
  Tag Maturity Level
  Tag Status (Operational / Void)

Design and Process Attributes include but not limited to the following:

  Design Pressure (Max) - Cold Side
  Design Pressure (Max) - Hot Side
  Design Pressure (Min) - Cold Side
  Design Pressure (Min) - Hot Side…

Required Document Types include but not limited to the following:

  P&ID
  Line List
  Process / Mechanical Data Sheets
Instrument Index & Data Sheets

  Cause and Effect
  Loop Diagram
  Maintenance Instruction Manual…

Tag Attributes are typically classified by their Functional and Physical designation
whereas Documents attributes are typically referred to by the term Meta-data which
include but not limited to the following:

  Document Number
  Document Title
  Document Revision
  Document Issue Date
  Document Issue Status
  Document Filename
  Discipline
  Export Control Classification
  Purchase Order (i.e. for Physical Supplier documents only)

5.3.1  Functional Attributes

Functional attributes are generally the technical design requirements for the tagged
item, for example the maximum design pressure of a pump and functional data is
normally available when the design engineer has developed the datasheet for the
equipment.

5.3.1.1  Inheritance of Attributes

Only classes defined as “leaf end classes” may be instantiated in an application. Their
parent classes may still have Attribute Sets allocated to them which allows propagation
of Attribute Sets down the hierarchy as required. For example, an Attribute Set called
“Process Common” could be defined for the “Process Equipment” class. Its Attributes
would then be available to child classes such as “Agitator”, “Fan”, etc.

AVEVA Information Engineering

KBA-IE-210-005

Specialisation classes and the Attribute inheritance denoted by the following colour
key:




 (Orange) - Parent Tag attributes
 (Blue) - Tag attribute inheritance

5.3.2  Physical Attributes

The physical piece of Equipment that is installed on the plant is uniquely identified by
its Model / Serial Number and is individually characterised by its physical attributes
that specify its operating and performance specification. Physical attributes include but
are not limited to the following:

  Supplier and/or OEM name
  Model Number / Part Number
  Serial Number
Instillation Date

  Warranty End Date
  Spare Part information
  Dimension - Length X Width X Depth (Installed)
  Weight - Dry (Installed)
  Weight - Operating (Installed)

5.4  Units of Measure (UoM)
The Class Library4 include a comprehensive set of Measure Classes as shown below in
the ISM ‘Units of Measure’ tab. The Measure Classes provide a logical grouping such
that the combinations of <Pressure>-<Pressure Classes>-<Measure Unit>-<Attribute>-
<Functional Class> can be managed and seamlessly navigated.

Issued

Page 15 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

5.5  Disciplines
The use of the Discipline classification introduces a logical grouping or sub-division
that prescribes the ownership of Tag classes and is founded on industry best practice.
The Class Library4 naming of Disciplines identifies the widely used main Discipline
groups that includes but not limited to the following:

  Equipment - Electrical
  Equipment - Drilling
  Equipment - Instrument
  Equipment - Static
  Equipment - HVAC
  Equipment - Safety and Rescue
  Equipment - Piping
  Equipment - Mechanical
  Equipment - Package
  Equipment - Rotating
  Equipment - Subsea

The use of Discipline allows the rapid searching for particular Tag classes due to the
logical ownership taxonomy, additionally the use of the Discipline provides the
mechanism for the generation of Discipline based report outputs.

5.6  Associations
Associations provide the mechanisms to rapidly locate and retrieve Tags, Documents,
Drawings, Parts lists and other technical information stored in the Owners EDW and
typically include the following object combinations;

  Tag-Tag
  Tag parent / child connections (From/To)
  Tag-Document (Functional / Physical)
  Doc-Doc

Whilst there are other association types these are the main object – object associations.

Issued

Page 16 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

6  ISO 15926 (Part 4) Reference Data Library (RDL)
The following rules will be applied to the creation of Classes and Attributes:

6.1.1  Class and Attributes

  Class and Attribute names held in the RDL conforms to ISO 15926 part 4 and

RDLs are given the Reference Data System (RDS) number.

  Where possible all functional class names will be mapped to their equivalent

held within the RDL of ISO 15926 part 4.

o  Class names which are not in ISO 15926 (part 4) will be reviewed by the
Information Engineering group and created from the oil and gas ‘de
facto’ name according to their equipment description.

  Attributes names which are not in ISO 15926 (part 4) are reviewed and created

according to their attribute functional description and associated to their
respective equipment class according to the oil and gas de facto name such as]

o  Relief Temperature, Signal Mode etc.

  There shall be no duplications of Classes or Attributes

Each class name will have class properties which will include:

  Unique ID
  RDS number (if the class can be mapped to the ISO 15926 Part 4 RDL)
  Description (Where possible the description in line with ISO 15926 Part 4 RDL)
  Owning Class Discipline

Each attribute will have properties which will include:

  Unique ID
  RDS number (if the class can be mapped to the ISO 15926 Part 4 RDL)
  Description (Where possible the description in line with ISO 15926 Part 4 RDL
  Date Created (The date the new object in the Class Library4 was created)
  Originating source (e.g. ISO 15926)
  Value validation rules (Data Type, UOM, and Association etc.)

The RDL model will be based on ISO 15926 part 4 and will include the following
objects:

  Tagged objects
  System Number
  Model Number
  Discipline
  Document Name
  Resource / Equipment Number

The AVEVA Class Library4 will model the engineering objects by their:

  Functional Tag number or physical Model/Serial Number properties and include
  A Functional Class hierarchal structure based on the Discipline, Parent Class,

Specialisation classes, UoM and validation criteria4

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

7  Implementation of the Class Library?
The Class Library4 is based on industry standards and provides a foundation on which
the Owners information standard and their criteria for the information governance are
developed. Due to the differences in corporate, regional and national requirements
there will differing priorities as a result of business systems and working practices
from Owner to Owner and these need to be accommodated in their respective
handover standards. Whilst the standards on which Class Libraries are based have been
long established they can still be subject to change.

The illustration below shows how an Owner would take the Class Library4 and the
activities to take the baseline release and incorporate their own corporate
requirements which is primarily related to the incorporation of the Owners Tag and
Document Numbering Specifications (ENS8 and DNS9) and other related documentation.

Owners Naming

Lists, Schedules,
Specs...

Information
Handover

End

The primary activity will be to align the Tag and Document classes defined in the Class
Library4 to those defined in the Owners reference documents; the Tag8 and Document9
naming specifications.

Issued

Page 18 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

These specifications define the Owners standardised numbering system for assigning
unique Tag and Document numbers and identification codes to plant areas and
modules for design, fabrication, operations and maintenance purposes. There are no
requirements to manage Tag and Document Classes not addressed in these
documents.

Having aligned the Class Library4 classes to the ENS8 and DNS9 a similar exercise is
undertaken by defining the Attribute requirements for Tags and Documents classes.
These attributes are contained in a variety of project documents and business systems
including but not limited to the following;

  Design Specifications
  Pipe Specifications
  Valve Specifications
  Data Sheet templates
  Line Lists
  Cable Schedules
  Alarm and Trip schedules
  3rd party systems:
o  SAP
o  Maximo
o  Others…

These project documents and business system requirements also provide a clearer
view of the Document associations and Parent/Child relationships which may be
explicitly stated or implied from the information dependencies.

Once the attributes have been mapped to the appropriate Classes and potential
duplications of both Classes and Attributes have been resolved and the appropriate
UoMs have been mapped to the Attributes the Class Library4 will be issued to the
Owner for final approval.

Upon approval the Class Library4 will be configured in the Owners EDW and used to
validate the handover information.

8  Navigating the Class Library
The structure of the Class Library4 and the functionality of ISM provides a completely
recursive environment enabling the user to bi-directionally navigate the Class Library4
between Classes, Attributes, Disciplines, and UoM by means of the associativity model.
The screenshots below show examples of the Class Library4 content with the hierarchy
breakdown for searching and navigation to the left of the page and the detail page to
the right with all of the navigable associations.

functionality of ISM provide the mechanisms to view and manage:

o  Functional
o  Document

  Design Documents
  Supplier Documents

  Discipline
  Document Type
  Attributes Group

o  ATEX, Electrical attributes etc.

  Measure Class
  Measure Units

8.5  Units of Measure
The Unit of Measure stores the current SI units and is associated to the Measure Class
(e.g. Barg - Pressure). The Units of Measure are defined in the Class Library4 worksheet
and uses the abbreviation of the Unit of Measure based on industry standard ISO 1000
(SI Units) and the UoM have been associated to their respective attribute.

The operator that determines whether or not a UoM is required is a Y / N Boolean:




“YES” - UoM is required (ticked boxes in the ‘UoM Required’ column)
“NO” - UoM are not required (unticked boxes in the ‘UoM Required’ column)

8.6  Document Classes and Metadata (Attributes)
The Class Library4 uses the following classifications for Documents:

  Project Design Documents
  Project Supplier Documents

Each document class is displayed in the hierarchy browser under its classification and
includes the document meta-data requirements for each document, the attribute for
which are defined in the Class Library4 document worksheet.

10  Class Library Support Services

10.1 Class Library Consultancy
Provision of a consultancy service to capture customers’ information management
requirements and deliver a fit for purpose, Corporate and/or project specific Class
Library4 that meets the requirements of the project and their contractors. This will
based on a version of the Class Library4.

Where modification to the existing dataset or additive changes are required as a result
of customers’ requirements or working practices these will be updated into the
customer’s specific version of the Class Library4 to ensure uniqueness of the evolving
dataset.

10.2 Class Library Updates
The Class Library4 update process is described in detail in Ref [2], Generally the
obligation to support and maintain the components of the Class Library4 shall be the
remit of the ‘Information Engineering Group’ who will assume responsibility for the
following remedial actions:

  Maintaining the dataset in line with latest industry standard on which it is based

including ISO and other standard updates

  The frequency of updates will be dependent upon the frequency of changes to

the underlying standards

  Updates undertaken as a result of client project updates or EPC request for

change

  Support of clients specific Class Library4 implementations on a project to project

basis.

10.3 Management and Publication
The release mechanism for the Class Library4 will be the Information Standard Manager
ISM provides the functionality that allows class libraries and their content to be
managed, maintained, copied, updated, compared and versioned for exported in a
number of formats to suit the receiving application.

Configuration of the content to be included or excluded in the release version is
controlled using the ‘Export Class Library’ mechanism, this provides the ability for the
user to include specific aspects of the content and the audit and traceability criteria
shown in the image below:

Issued

Page 26 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

10.4 Quality Assurance and Testing
Formalised internal testing of updates or modifications to the Class Library4 will be
undertaken by AVEVA who will ensure the integrity and compliance with the relevant
standards on which the Class Library4 is based.

Responsibility of testing the updated Class Library4 with the Owners business systems
and acceptance of the updates will be as per the Owners acceptance process.

Issued

Page 27 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

11  Appendix A

11.1 Supporting Standards
Aspects of the following standards have been incorporated into the Class Library4 and
particular emphasis has been placed on ensuring that duplication or contradiction
across sources has been resolved. The main industry standards use to guide the
content of the Class Library4 includes but is not limited to the following:

11.1.1  Evolution of ISO 15926 (Part 4)

Parts (4) and (5) of this globally recognised standard prescribes the criteria for the
‘Integration and exchange of plant data throughout the life of the plant’ and in
particular ‘the integration of design data from different design systems and the
handover of design data from design contractor to owner/operator’.

  Part (4) describes the hierarchal structures and the naming of Classes and

attributes and their parent child relationships.

  Part (5) described the methods and working practices to maintain Part 4 in a

database format

In total, ISO 15926 consists of 13 parts, Part 4 has evolved over a number of years and
was driven by the industry need for a consistent method for defining the terms used
within facilities for the process industry in a reference data context.

Since 1991 a number of professional bodies including ESPRIT, EPISTLE, PISTEP,
POSC/Caesar and USPI and industry standards including AP221, ISO1303 and
ISO15926-2 have combined, merged and evolved to become today’s ISO 15926 (Part
4).

11.1.2  ISO 14224

ISO 14224:2006 provides a comprehensive basis for the collection of reliability and
maintenance data in a standard format for equipment in all facilities and operations
within the petroleum, natural gas and petrochemical industries during the operational
life cycle of equipment.

It describes terms and definitions that constitute a "reliability language" that describes
failure modes and recommends a minimum amount of data that is required to be
collected and focuses on the two main issues:

  Data requirements for the type of data to be collected for use in various analysis

methodologies and

  Standardized data format to facilitate the exchange of reliability and

maintenance data between plants, owners, manufacturers and contractors.

11.1.3  CFIHOS

CFIHOS (Capital Facilities Information HandOver Specification) provides a practical
implementation of the ISO 15926 standard and provides standards, methods and a
Reference Data Library for owner operators to specify the engineering information they
require for future operations and maintenance of the facilities.

Issued

Page 28 of 29

AVEVA Information Engineering
Class Library Reference Document
KBA-IE-210-005

11.1.4  Class Library and Document Versions/Revisions

The release versions of the Class Library4 and this document will be maintained in
parallel and Class Library4 releases will necessitate updates to this guidance document.

Expectations of a digital transformation program

▪ Improve process & product quality and robustness
▪ Increase level of standardization, to facilitate training of operators and reach constant product & process quality
▪ Have all data on hands, in real time, to decide, to anticipate and understand root causes
▪ Reduce the nonvalue added times
▪ Build a strong organization & governance to monitor the transformation – deliver & Show benefits

What’s next ?

Thanks to the easily accessible and standardized data structure (MES, Historian, etc.) within the Digital Transformation
program, we will be able to develop data-driven use cases, including data visualization (Performance Dashboard,
Benchmarking) and Artificial Intelligence to maximize benefits at the group level.

Predictive maintenance
Analyzing historical data with AI
algorithms to identify patterns and
trends, identifying weak signals,
enabling proactive maintenance to
prevent equipment failures.

Benchmarking:
Identifying the best-performing
machines at Nexans based on
credible data, understand the
reasons for their success, and
encourage knowledge sharing.

Generative AI:
Leveraging the benefits of diverse
data (8D process, diverse
languages, local SWI, etc.) to
enhance Industry 4.0 performance
and security with minimal time and
effort.

Performance Dashboard
Establishing indicators to facilitate
performance improvement and
identify continuous improvement
opportunities as well as workshop
animation.

To be continued

<!-- Slide number: 1 -->
# Perspectives on Digital Twin Within the Detailed Design Phase
Enhancing design accuracy through digital integration
1

![Picture Placeholder 10](PicturePlaceholder10.jpg)

### Notes:
Good morning/afternoon ladies and gentlemen. Thank you all for joining us today for this insightful conference on “Perspectives on Digital Twin Within the Detailed Design Phase." My name is [Your Name], and I am [Your Position] at Ramboll.
It's a pleasure to see such a diverse group of professionals gathered here to explore the innovative applications of digital twin technology in the engineering domain.

Good morning and good afternoon, ladies and gentlemen.
Thank you for joining me today, where I’ll share some perspectives on Digital Twin technology within the detailed design phase of a project.
My name is Christian Lindquist, and I am the Digital Transition Lead within my department, which focuses on offshore substations. I am also the Product Owner of our CAD system, called AVEVA.
It’s a pleasure to see such a diverse group of professionals gathered here to explore the innovative applications of digital twin technology in the engineering domain.

<!-- Slide number: 2 -->
# Agenda Highlights
Current and Future Data Management and Presentation for Deliverables
The Role and Significance of Single Point Of Data Entry (SPODE)
Integration of 1D, 2D, and 3D Data in the Design Phase
Ensuring Asset Management and Tagging Quality Assurance
Seamless Integration of Third-Party Systems in the Design Phase
Integration with Client Systems and Fulfilling Digital Deliveries Requirements
2

### Notes:
Today, I’ll walk you through a few key topics — areas where I’ll take a bit of a stand — to highlight how we, in offshore substation topside projects, are moving toward a more digital and data-driven world.
We’re transitioning from traditional design phases and deliverable-based workflows to a new digital era, where we work increasingly with databases and interconnected information. The goal is to create a streamlined environment where data is accessible and reusable across teams and disciplines.

<!-- Slide number: 3 -->
# Current and Future Data Management and Presentation for Deliverables
3

### Notes:
The first topic I’d like to cover is how we currently — and in the future — handle data management, and how that impacts our deliverables.
Let’s begin by looking at our current approach to data management and presentation during the detailed design phase, using offshore substations as an example.
Efficient data management is crucial for developing accurate, timely, and high-quality deliverables.
In our current design process, we have multiple engineering disciplines that must coordinate, interact, and collaborate closely. Communication typically happens through meetings and emails, and data is stored in a variety of locations — for instance, in document control systems, SharePoint sites, and local folders.
Each discipline — and sometimes each engineer — has their own way of doing things. As a result, we often end up with a large number of deliverables, all of which need to be coordinated across disciplines.
And in many cases, the same data appears in multiple deliverables, leading to duplication and inconsistency.

# Normal design process

### Notes:
In our current design process, we have multiple engineering disciplines that must coordinate, interact, and collaborate closely. Communication typically happens through meetings and emails, and data is stored in a variety of locations — for instance, in document control systems, SharePoint sites, and local folders.
Each discipline — and sometimes each engineer — has their own way of doing things. As a result, we often end up with a large number of deliverables, all of which need to be coordinated across disciplines.
And in many cases, the same data appears in multiple deliverables, leading to duplication and inconsistency.

<!-- Slide number: 5 -->
# Application level
5

![Picture 2](Picture2.jpg)

### Notes:
This I what we call SPODE

<!-- Slide number: 6 -->
# Normal design process
Challenges:
Fragmented Data Source
Manual workflows
Inconsistet Data Formats
Accessuility Issues
6
Result:
Duplicate effort in data entry
Increased likelihood for errors
Delayed project timelines

### Notes:
Challenges:
Today, our data management processes face several challenges that can impact the reliability and accessibility of information.
Fragmented Data Sources: Information is often stored across multiple systems and formats, making it difficult to consolidate and retrieve.
Inconsistent Data Formats: Data can come in various formats, from textual documents to complex simulations, without a standardization procedure.
Accessibility Issues: Retrieving specific data can be time-consuming and prone to errors due to lack of proper indexing and centralization.

Result:
These challenges often result in inefficiencies such as:
Duplicate efforts in data entry and verification.
Increased likelihood of errors and inconsistencies.
Delayed project timelines due to prolonged data consolidation and validation processes.

In our current or “normal” design process, we face several challenges that impact the reliability and accessibility of information.
As we saw before, our data sources are fragmented. Information is stored across multiple systems and formats, which makes it difficult to consolidate and retrieve.
Most of these processes are handled manually, which increases the risk of errors, delays, and inconsistencies in our deliverables.
We also deal with inconsistent data formats — information might appear as text in Word documents, numbers in Excel sheets, or visual data in CAD models, each in different file types and structures.
Then there are accessibility issues. Retrieving specific data can be time-consuming and error-prone due to the lack of proper indexing or centralized storage, even when we use document control systems.
All these factors lead to inefficiencies, such as:
- Duplicated efforts in data entry and verification
- Higher risk of errors and inconsistencies
- Delays in project timelines caused by lengthy data consolidation and validation processes

<!-- Slide number: 7 -->
# One small error – huge impact

![Content Placeholder 5](ContentPlaceholder5.jpg)
7

### Notes:
A good example of how errors can impact the engineering and design process is when a temperature change is either not recorded correctly or not updated in the proper documentation.
If other disciplines, engineers, or mechanical completion teams are not informed about this change, that seemingly minor mistake can have a significant ripple effect throughout the project.
That small temperature deviation can trigger a chain reaction —the pipe stress calculations need to be reassessed,the P&IDs must be updated,and even specifications or installation classes might need modification.
Those updates then have to be reflected in the 3D model, which could in turn create clashes with other equipment or systems.
And if the pipeline has already been ordered or construction has begun, the team may need to reorder materials or even redo part of their work.
All of this, within the traditional design phase, is handled manually — making it both inefficient and highly prone to error.

# Datawarehouse & digital deliveries

### Notes:
With that in mind, let’s take a look into the future —a future where tools like Excel, CAD drawings, extracts, and Word documents still exist,but they are no longer the source of our data.
In this future, all data originates from a central data warehouse —a data-centric environment that serves as a single source of truth for the entire project.
This warehouse is accessible to all stakeholders — engineers, designers, project managers, and others — regardless of discipline.
From this data warehouse, we can still generate project deliverables such as drawings, lists, and reports,but the key difference is that everyone works from the same live dataset.
As shown in this diagram, the connections are now simplified.There’s no longer direct communication between deliverables — instead, the communication and data exchange happen within the data warehouse itself.
This creates a more streamlined, transparent, and consistent way of managing project information.
